name: Grizzlethorn Encampment
description: A displaced goblin tribe has claimed the ironwood thicket at the edge of the Whispering Woods, raiding trade carts to survive after being driven from their mountain territory. They are not conquerors — but they are desperate, and desperate things bite.

stages.title: The Overgrown Path
stages.description: The trail into the thicket narrows to single file as the ironwood closes in — and something moves in the branches above.

successEffect.narrative: The party spots the Thornwalkers before they loose their first volley. The fight begins on the party's terms.

failureEffect.narrative: The party senses something but can't place it. Three arrows find gaps in armor before anyone shouts a warning.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Thornwalkers watch the party walk the path below them like they've watched a hundred caravans. Nobody looks up.

successEffect.narrative: Someone in the party knows this land — the old halfling paths through the roots lead around the first ambush entirely.

failureEffect.narrative: The memory is there but too vague. The path forks and the party picks wrong.

successEffect.narrative: Nothing. Probably a bird.

failureEffect.narrative: A tripwire catches an ankle. Not serious — just a reminder the goblins have had weeks to prepare this ground.

stages.title: The Encampment
stages.description: The trade-canvas tents of the Grizzlethorn are packed together in a clearing — movement is difficult and the Bone Elders are already chanting.

successEffect.narrative: One party member slips through the tent flaps and catches a Bone Elder mid-chant. One less curse to fight through.

failureEffect.narrative: The attempt is spotted. The Elder finishes the chant before the party can close the gap.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The party has no way to slip past quietly. The Elders are mid-chant before the first blow lands.

successEffect.narrative: The party notices the fear in the Scrappers' eyes. These aren't raiders by choice. The hesitation costs the goblins the first exchange.

failureEffect.narrative: The party sees only enemies. The fight is harder for it.

stages.title: Chief Grik's Hideout
stages.description: The largest tent in the encampment is reinforced with stolen timber and iron spikes. Chief Grik is already on his feet.

successEffect.narrative: The trophies on the tent poles aren't trophies — they're belongings. Someone recognizes what they are. Grik notices the pause. The fight begins a breath slower.

failureEffect.narrative: The tent is dark. Nobody sees anything except a very large goblin with an axe.

successEffect.narrative: The tent is empty. Grik drops from the ceiling.

failureEffect.narrative: Grik was behind the tent flap. The first swing connects before anyone is ready.

lore.text: The halfling families who hunted this wood remember it differently. They call it the Greywood now, and none of them will say why.
Grik's tribe was called the Stoneback Clan before the displacement. The mountain they came from has no name on any surface map.
A merchant who survived a Grizzlethorn raid swore they only took food. Never coin, never weapons. Just food.

name: Stone Tooth Pass
description: A narrow mountain defile locked in a four-year dwarven succession war. Two degraded armies and a band of stranded merchants fight over the only safe route through the Godspine range. The party must choose a path through the pass — and deal with whoever controls it.

stages.title: The Scree Approach
stages.description: The approach to the pass is littered with abandoned supply wagons and skirmishing soldiers. Both factions are too busy bleeding each other to notice the party — until they do.

successEffect.narrative: Your party spots the caltrop fields and loose-rock triggers before stepping into them, skirting the ambush entirely and entering the fight from a flanking position.

failureEffect.narrative: A loose-rock trigger catches the party off guard. Stones cascade from the ridge, wounding everyone before the fighting even begins.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without anyone skilled in reading terrain, the party walks straight into the caltrop fields. The Scouts are already laughing when the real fight starts.

successEffect.narrative: A stranded merchant waves the party down before they enter the killing ground. She sketches the patrol routes in the dirt — the party enters the fight knowing exactly where everyone is.

failureEffect.narrative: A merchant calls out a warning, but the party can't parse her frantic gesturing in time. They nod politely and walk straight into the skirmish line.

successEffect.narrative: The timing is perfect — the soldiers are distracted.

failureEffect.narrative: The party crests the rise just as a stray boulder from a rockslide rolls through the approach. Nobody is seriously hurt, but everyone is shaken.

successEffect.narrative: The cliff face above the defile has a ledge route — not obvious, but passable with rope. You come down behind the checkpoint. One of the Goldcrown scouts is still looking at the road when you arrive on the wrong side of them.

failureEffect.narrative: The pass narrows to a single chokepoint. Both armies are watching it.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The pass narrows to a single chokepoint. Both armies are watching it.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The lower tunnels are accessible — the Ironvein hold this ground.

stages.title: The Lower Tunnels
stages.description: The Ironvein Loyalists hold the cave system beneath the pass — their shield walls fill every chokepoint and they fight with the grim endurance of men who have not retreated in four years.

successEffect.narrative: The party finds a secondary passage behind the shield wall and collapses it, cutting off reinforcements. One fewer Conscript joins the fight.

failureEffect.narrative: The party attempts to flank through a side tunnel and finds it occupied. They fight their way back to the main passage, bloodied.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The tunnels are a maze to those who don't know them. The Loyalists hear the party coming from three corridors away.

successEffect.narrative: Your party's bearing gives the Loyalists pause. For a moment, the Stone-Guards hesitate — these are not the soft merchants they usually ambush. They readjust their formation, buying the party a breath.

failureEffect.narrative: The Loyalists take one look at the party and tighten their shields. They've seen enough desperation to recognize it.

stages.title: The High Ledges
stages.description: The Goldcrown Usurpers command the watchtowers and cliffside positions above the pass — a paranoid, rain-soaked garrison that shoots first and never asks questions.

successEffect.narrative: One party member doubles back and approaches the tower from the blind side, putting a Scout out of the fight before the alarm is raised.

failureEffect.narrative: The deception is almost convincing. Almost. The Scout's horn sounds while the party is still in the open.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The party has no way to approach without being seen. The Scouts loose two volleys before the party can close the distance.

successEffect.narrative: The party charges the ledge position without breaking stride. The Scouts, accustomed to prey that runs, hesitate just long enough to lose their best shot.

failureEffect.narrative: The bold advance telegraphs itself too clearly. The Scouts adjust their aim and the first volley finds its mark.

stages.title: The War Council Cavern
stages.description: At the heart of the pass, Warlord Thorin Ironvein has cornered a Goldcrown delegation in the cavern shrine of the mountain ancestors. Both factions turn on the intruders without hesitation.

successEffect.narrative: Someone in the party notices the unfinished warhammer at Thorin's belt — half-crafted, clearly not his. For a moment the Warlord's eyes go somewhere else. The fight begins a breath slower.

failureEffect.narrative: Thorin takes one look at the party and raises his hammer. Whatever he sees, it isn't reason enough to pause.

successEffect.narrative: The party arrives in better shape than expected.

failureEffect.narrative: The last tunnel collapses a section of ceiling as the party enters the cavern. Dust and rock — not enough to stop anyone, but enough to remind them the mountain is not neutral.

lore.text: The ward-stones lining the Godspine were placed by Khaz-Morad engineers three centuries ago. The dwarves who know how to repair them died in the first year of the war.
Elara Stonehand was a grain merchant before the blockade. She has not left the pass in two years. She does not talk about going home anymore.
Thorin Ironvein has turned down three separate offers of safe passage. Nobody knows if he is waiting for a sign from the mountain or if he simply has nowhere left to go.

name: Sunken Sanctum of Aethelgard
description: Three hundred years ago the city of Aethelgard sank in a single night, cursed by a ritual meant to bind a water spirit to human will. The survivors made a religion of the disaster. They still live beneath the lake, breathing through ancient wards, and they mean to drown the rest of the world.

stages.title: The Sunken Shore
stages.description: Broken bridges and half-submerged islands mark the approach to the Sanctum — and something moves beneath the dark water.

successEffect.narrative: The subtle displacement of bioluminescent moss reveals where the Scavengers wait beneath the surface. The party is ready when they breach.

failureEffect.narrative: The first sign of the ambush is claws closing on ankles. The Scavengers drag two party members half into the water before they can react.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The dark water reveals nothing to those who do not know how to look. The party is already fighting when the Scavengers surface.

stages.title: The Flooded Cathedral
stages.description: The nave of Aethelgard's great cathedral stands half-submerged, its pillars draped in coral and its shadows full of soldiers who have never known dry land.

successEffect.narrative: Moving through the cathedral's side passages, your party flanks the Deep Chanters before they can begin their hymns. One falls silently. The Guards are already weaker for it.

failureEffect.narrative: A misplaced step disturbs the silt. The Chanters' hymns swell to fill the cathedral before the party can close, and the Guards' shells seem to thicken in response.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The party walks into the cathedral openly. The Chanters begin singing immediately, and by the time the first blow lands, every Guard in the hall has been blessed.

stages.title: The Altar of the Spirit
stages.description: At the heart of the Sanctum stands the altar where Aethelgard's founders attempted their ritual — and where Archbishop Valerius has spent three centuries completing it.

lore.text: The ritual that sank Aethelgard was not meant to bind the spirit. The original text called it an invitation. The spirit accepted.
The Tidebound Penitents keep no written records. They say that writing things down is how lies become history.
Archbishop Valerius has not spoken in eleven years. His congregation insists he communicates through the water. Visitors who stay long enough begin to agree.

name: Whispering Willow Shrine
description: A forgotten shrine deep in the shallow valley, where minor nature spirits have claimed the abandoned altar of a dissolved monastic order. They mean no harm — but they guard this ground as if their existence depends on it, because it does.

lore.text: The Order of the Verdant Word dissolved not from heresy but from debt. Their final records show a tithe unpaid for eleven years.
The willow circle was not planted. It grew on its own around the altar in a single season, two years after the monks left.
The Heart-Wisp does not remember the monks. It remembers the prayers they left behind, still warm in the stone.
The altar does not mark where the monks prayed. It marks where they stopped the thing beneath from coming up. The prayer was a lock, not a gift.

The willow circle is not Verdant Word work. The Order blessed what was already here. The original tree was planted by an elf — one of the Silverbough Elders who walked the Vale's roads before the Order existed, leaving markers at sites where the land's nature-frequency was strong enough to anchor. The monks found the marker and built around it. They never knew what they were building around. The Gallery of Fractured Light knows this place still.

stages.title: The Outer Grove
stages.description: The willow circle begins here — trailing branches brush the path and small lights dart between the roots, watching.

successEffect.narrative: The inscriptions on the path stones are a greeting, not a warning. Someone in the party traces the symbols and the nearest Sparkling dims — curious rather than alarmed.

failureEffect.narrative: The symbols mean something, but the reading is wrong. The Sparklings flare bright and a root catches an ankle before the party steadies itself.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The path stones are covered in old script nobody can read. The spirits take the ignorance as disrespect and the branches close in.

successEffect.narrative: The party moves through the outer grove without disturbing the grass. The Sparklings orbit at a distance, weighing whether these visitors mean harm. They haven't decided yet.

failureEffect.narrative: Someone snaps a branch. The grove goes quiet for a moment, then very loud.

successEffect.narrative: The mist parts unexpectedly, drawn back by a change in the wind.

failureEffect.narrative: The morning mist rolls in thicker than expected. Someone walks face-first into a willow branch they didn't see coming.

successEffect.narrative: At the treeline beyond the willow circle, something is watching. Not the wisps — something still and deliberate, at a height that suggests it is standing in the lower branches rather than on the ground. It does not advance. When you look directly at it, it is gone. The absence it leaves is the specific absence of something that chose to leave rather than something that was never there.

failureEffect.narrative: The treeline offers nothing to those without the right frequency.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The willow circle is quiet.

stages.title: The Inner Shrine
stages.description: The altar at the center of the willow circle hums with residual prayer. The Heart-Wisp drifts above it, and the Mist-Callers have already begun their work.

successEffect.narrative: Someone places something at the altar's base — it doesn't matter what, only that it was given freely. One of the Mist-Callers stills, drawn to examine the gift, and does not return to the fight.

failureEffect.narrative: The gesture is there but the intent reads as hollow. The Heart-Wisp pulses once, and the mist thickens in response.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Nobody in the party knows the old forms of greeting. The Heart-Wisp has been waiting two hundred years for someone who does.

successEffect.narrative: The altar responds to the party's presence — a faint warmth in the stone, a brief cessation of the hum. The Heart-Wisp tilts, uncertain. It has not felt this in a long time.

failureEffect.narrative: The altar is just stone to those who cannot hear it. The Heart-Wisp makes no distinction between them and the last ten parties who tried to take its flowers.

successEffect.narrative: The altar responds to nature-attuned hands and a spirit in deep harmony with the land. Something is sealed here — old, deliberate, and deep. You can feel the mechanism of it. The way down exists. You haven't found the moment yet, but you will.

failureEffect.narrative: You sense the lock but cannot read it. The harmony required is beyond what the party carries today.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The altar is cold stone to those without nature-attunement. Whatever is beneath it does not acknowledge you.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The lock yields. Something that has been sealed for two hundred years opens. The party descends.

stages.title: The Roots Below
stages.description: Beneath the altar, the roots of the willow circle descend into a chamber that predates the shrine by centuries. The air is cold. The thing that was locked here is awake.

name: The Moonlit Ferry Crossing
description: A mist-shrouded river crossing where the ferry still runs — crewed by those who never left. The toll has not changed. The patience of the crew has.

lore.text: The ferry runs whether you board it or not. It has been running for thirty years without a living hand on the oar.
Bram the ferryman died owing Lyra three seasons of song. She has been waiting for payment ever since.
The halfling communities upstream stopped coming to the river the year Bram died. They say the water sounds different now. They are not wrong.
Lyra does not hate the living. She hates that they forgot. There is a difference, and it matters, and she is the only one left who knows it.

stages.title: The Riverbank Approach
stages.description: Willows crowd the bank and the water catches moonlight it has no right to catch at this hour. The fog is not natural. Neither are the shapes moving in it.

successEffect.narrative: You place a halfling coin on the dock post. One of the shapes in the fog pauses, regards it, and drifts back into the water. The others seem less certain of you now.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing to offer. The fog thickens.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The crossing does not acknowledge you. It only knows those who pay.

successEffect.narrative: The river is not quiet — it is listening. You catch the shape of an old song in the current, half-remembered. The Deckhands drift away from you slightly, as if uncertain.

failureEffect.narrative: The water tells you nothing. Or perhaps you are not listening the right way.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Silverstream does not speak to those who have not earned its attention.

stages.title: The Ferry Dock
stages.description: The dock extends into the Silverstream on rotting timbers that should not hold. The ferry sits motionless at the end of it. The Ferryman's Echo waits at the prow, watching.

successEffect.narrative: The tarnished bell at the dock's end catches your eye — its surface is carved with halfling river-script. The characters glow faintly when you touch them. Beneath the grime: a name. Lyra. And beneath that: a verse of the old song, half worn away.

failureEffect.narrative: The bell is old and tarnished. You cannot make out the markings on its surface.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The bell hangs silent. Whatever it once said has been forgotten.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The verse on the bell is incomplete but you know enough to finish it. The water stirs. Something older than Bram rises from the deep.

stages.title: The River's Memory
stages.description: The water pulls back from the dock to reveal a sandbar that should not exist. At its center, Lyra — the river spirit herself, everything that was patient and kind about this place before the halflings stopped singing to her. She is not patient anymore.

name: The Vulture's Perch
description: A crumbling Royal Guard watchtower above the King's Road, occupied by the Vulture Company — deserters, rogues, and former soldiers who decided the road was worth more than the men guarding it.

lore.text: The Vulture Company did not start as bandits. Most of them were soldiers. Some of them were good ones.
The tower has been abandoned by the Royal Guard for forty years. No one at the capital has ever offered an explanation for why the garrison vanished. The official record lists them as deserters.

Captain Vane has held this tower for six years. In that time, three Royal Guard scouting parties have approached and turned back. He says they recognized his commission seal. He says they still respect it. No one else believes him.

The garrison did not desert. They found the vault records and sent a courier to the capital. The courier never arrived. Three weeks later the garrison was gone. The records are still here. So is whatever made the capital want them buried.

stages.title: The Outer Courtyard
stages.description: The courtyard is a mess of salvaged barricades, spent crossbow bolts, and the bones of old skirmishes. The Scrappers watch from the walls. The Roadwardens are already moving.

successEffect.narrative: The caltrop field at the gate is barely hidden — someone swept it too quickly. You step around it and the Scrapper watching from the wall doesn't realize you saw him. One fewer pair of eyes on the entry.

failureEffect.narrative: You catch the glint of metal too late. The caltrops slow you down before the fight even starts.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The courtyard is a mess of debris. It's hard to tell what's deliberate and what's neglect.

successEffect.narrative: A flagstone near the old guardhouse sits slightly proud of the others. Under it: a key on a chain, old iron, stamped with the Royal Guard crest. Whoever hid it here did so in a hurry.

failureEffect.narrative: You search the courtyard but find nothing except evidence that it has been picked clean by the current occupants.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The courtyard offers nothing useful to those who do not look carefully.

successEffect.narrative: You fix the rope to an outcrop above the main approach and come over the wall where the Roadwardens aren't watching. One of them is still looking at the road when the others start shouting.

failureEffect.narrative: The approach is guarded. You'll have to go through.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The approach is guarded. You'll have to go through.

stages.title: The Tower Keep
stages.description: The keep corridors are tight and smell of dogs and tallow. The warhounds hear you before you reach the top floor. The Veterans are already in position.

successEffect.narrative: There is a door at the base of the keep's stairwell that does not match the rest of the construction. Newer stone, older iron. Someone built this after the tower was already standing. The lock is Royal Guard standard issue.

failureEffect.narrative: The stairwell is plain stone. You head up toward the summit.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The keep interior is dark and unremarkable.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The key from the courtyard fits. The door opens inward. Below: stairs cut into the rock, and the smell of old paper and something else you cannot name.

stages.title: The Tower Summit
stages.description: The battlements are exposed to the wind and the long drop. Captain Vane waits at the far end with two Veterans flanking him. He has been expecting someone to come eventually.

successEffect.narrative: You call out before the fight starts. Vane holds up a hand and one of the Veterans steps back. 'State your business,' he says. Whatever you say next, the Veteran does not rejoin the line.

failureEffect.narrative: Vane looks at you for a moment, then nods to his men. 'Typical,' he says.

fallbackEffect.narrative: There is no negotiating with the summit. Only the wind and the drop.

stages.title: The Vault
stages.description: The vault is dry and cold and has not been properly opened since the garrison used it. The records are here — everything the garrison found, everything they reported, everything that was never answered. Vane is here too, reading them by candlelight. He does not look up when you enter.

name: The Blighted Orchard of Elder's Rest
description: A hillside apple orchard blessed by the Order of the Verdant Word two centuries ago, now slowly consuming itself. The caretakers remain. They are not themselves anymore.

lore.text: The Order of the Verdant Word did not bless places carelessly. Every site had a purpose. The orchard fed the healing houses of three valleys. Forty years of neglect was apparently enough to undo two centuries of care.
Sister Maren was not reckless. She was thorough. She spent four years studying the original blessing before she attempted the rite. She got almost everything right.

The Withered Choir does not believe it is suffering. This is the most disturbing thing about it. They sing while they work. The song is not quite the old Order hymns, but it is close enough that travelers who remember the old faith sometimes stop and listen before they realize what they are hearing.

The Order did not build the well. They built around it. Whatever the spring spirit was, the blessing was a negotiation, not a gift. Sister Maren did not know she was breaking a contract. She only knew the trees were dying.

The original blessing ritual that Sister Maren spent four years studying was not Verdant Word doctrine. It was a translation of an older text — elf work, from the Gallery of Fractured Light's teaching period, when the Silverbough Elders were still sharing their techniques with the Order's founders. The translation was imperfect. The elves knew it was imperfect. They did not correct it because by then they were already preparing to withdraw, and a slightly wrong blessing in a human orchard was not their problem to solve.

stages.title: The Outer Rows
stages.description: The apple trees grow in perfect rows that predate the blight by centuries. The fruit glows wrong. The Sproutlings move between the roots like they belong there, because now they do.

successEffect.narrative: Fire is what they fear. A controlled burn along the orchard's edge drives one cluster of Sproutlings back into the soil before the fight begins. The rest of the Choir goes still for a moment, as if listening to something underground.

failureEffect.narrative: The orchard is too wet from the blight to catch easily. The Choir does not even flinch.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The rows are quiet except for the sound of the fruit humming to itself.

successEffect.narrative: Someone in the party knows the old Order harvest hymn, or enough of it to hum the first verse. The Thorn-Weavers pause. The Choir responds from somewhere deeper in the orchard — not the right words, but the right tune. They are remembering something. It does not stop the fight but it slows the first approach.

failureEffect.narrative: The party has nothing to offer the Choir. The rows close in.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The orchard does not respond to strangers.

stages.title: The Central Grove
stages.description: The well at the orchard's center is stone gone grey-green with blight. The water that reaches the surface glows the same sickly color as the fruit. Sister Maren tends it with the attention of someone who has forgotten how to do anything else.

successEffect.narrative: The inscription around the well's lip is not about the orchard. It names the spirit inside the spring and the terms of the Order's agreement with it. The blight has grown over most of it but you can read enough. Something is still alive in the water beneath the corruption. Something that has been waiting.

failureEffect.narrative: The well's edge is carved with old script, too worn and overgrown to read.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The well is cold stone and blight. Nothing more.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The terms of the agreement are still binding. You speak them aloud at the well's edge. The blight recedes from the stone in a ring. The water below shifts color. Something rises.

stages.title: The Purified Spring
stages.description: Below the blight, something the Order sealed into the well two hundred years ago wakes up. It is not grateful. It kept the agreement. The agreement was broken without its consent. That is the part it has been thinking about for five years.

name: The Broken Axle Crossing
description: The only stone bridge across the ravine on the western trade road. The Goldcrown Usurpers hold it. Everything that moves between the mountains and the valley floor passes through their hands first.

lore.text: The bridge has a name carved into its capstone: Dunmore's Span. Nobody uses it. The man who built it has been dead for two hundred years and the Goldcrown Usurpers do not like landmarks that predate their claim.

Banner-Captain Kaelen keeps a precise ledger of everything seized at the checkpoint. Weight, origin, intended recipient, estimated value. She sends copies to the Goldcrown command at Stone Tooth Pass. Command has stopped acknowledging receipt.

Three Ironvein supply runners have attempted the crossing in the past year. Kaelen turned back two and seized one. The seized runner's cargo was grain. The ledger entry for it reads: 'Redistributed to unit provisions.' The grain went to her soldiers. She does not consider this theft.

The financial backing for the Goldcrown Usurpers does not come from within Khaz-Morad. The succession crisis is the surface. Below it: a human merchant consortium that profits from the trade blockade. Kaelen knows. The ledger she does not send to command is the one that matters.

stages.title: The Road Approach
stages.description: The trade road narrows where it meets the ravine. The Goldcrown scouts watch from the tree line. The caltrop field at the road's edge is freshly laid — this morning, by the look of it.

successEffect.narrative: You hold the Loyalist seal where the scouts can see it. One of them goes still. They are not supposed to engage Loyalist-credentialed parties without Banner-Captain authorization — that is the standing order. One scout pulls back to get that authorization. He will not return before the fight is over.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no papers. The scouts exchange a glance and reach for their weapons.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The checkpoint does not negotiate with uncredentialed travelers.

successEffect.narrative: The scout rotation has a gap — thirty seconds between the east watcher turning back and the west watcher reaching his position. You move through it. One fewer crossbow trained on the approach.

failureEffect.narrative: The checkpoint's coverage is tighter than it looks. You find no gap.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The bridge is watched from every angle. There is no quiet approach.

stages.title: The Bridge
stages.description: The bridge is thirty paces of open stone with nowhere to shelter. The Pike-Brothers fill the middle. The Crossbow Veterans cover from the far end. The formation is textbook. Kaelen trained them herself.

successEffect.narrative: The checkpoint records box sits on the bridge rail, unattended while the formation holds. A name catches your eye — a human merchant consortium, listed as a beneficiary of seized cargo. Not a dwarven name. Not a faction name. A trade house name. You note it before the Pike-Brothers advance.

failureEffect.narrative: There is no time to examine the checkpoint records before the formation moves.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The bridge offers no information, only opposition.

successEffect.narrative: The ravine wall has enough purchase for a rope descent. You come up on the far side while the Pike Brothers are watching the bridge approach. The crossbow veteran on the eastern parapet doesn't see you until it's too late to matter.

failureEffect.narrative: The ravine drops straight down on both sides. The bridge is the only crossing.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The ravine drops straight down on both sides. The bridge is the only crossing.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The Loyalist seal you carry is enough to get a message through to the supply runner sheltering in the ravine road. He emerges. Kaelen sends the interceptors.

stages.title: The Checkpoint Tower
stages.description: The tower at the bridge's far end is where Kaelen works. The door is open. She is already standing in it.

successEffect.narrative: Kaelen holds up a hand before you reach the door. 'You have thirty seconds,' she says. What you say in those thirty seconds determines whether one of the Pike-Brothers stands down. She is listening carefully.

failureEffect.narrative: Kaelen watches you approach and says nothing. The Pike-Brothers advance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The tower door is a position, not an invitation.

stages.title: The Diplomatic Escort
stages.description: The supply runner is a dwarf named Aldric who has been hiding in the ravine road for two days. Getting him across the bridge means fighting through the interceptors Kaelen has already dispatched. She did not come herself. She sent the ones she keeps for exactly this situation.

name: The Burnt Outpost
description: A Royal Guard watchtower on the Vale's western edge, destroyed five years ago when the garrison mage attempted to bind a fire spirit to the hearth. The mage survived. So did the fire. The garrison did not.

lore.text: The Royal Guard report lists the outpost as 'destroyed by fire of unknown origin' and the garrison as 'lost.' The mage is listed separately as 'deceased.' He is not.

The Ashen Remnant does not recruit. If you survive the explosion and the transformation, you are part of it. If you do not survive, you were not resilient enough. Captain Ignis considers this a reasonable selection process.

The fire spirit was already old when the mage found it. Old enough to remember when the Vale was forest and the Silver Vale was a swamp. Old enough to know what the Order of the Verdant Word was afraid of when they sealed certain sites. The mage did not ask it any of this. He only asked it to keep the hearth warm.

The binding did not fail. That is what no one outside this tower knows. The mage completed the ritual correctly. The fire spirit agreed to the terms. The explosion happened anyway — because something else wanted the spirit bound here, and used the mage's ritual to do it. The spirit has been trying to communicate this for five years. No one who reaches it knows how to listen.

stages.title: The Outer Courtyard
stages.description: The courtyard stones have been scorched and rescorched until they glow faintly at night. The Embers patrol the perimeter. The Flamekeepers watch from the broken battlements above.

successEffect.narrative: The coin is cold iron and old silver — enough of a conductor to draw a thread of water from the ground. Not much. Enough to dampen the courtyard's heat and give the Embers pause before they reach you.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing that would mean anything to fire.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The heat is immediate and total. There is no approaching quietly.

successEffect.narrative: You know fire well enough to know how it thinks. The Embers move in the pattern of a burn — outward from the center, always toward fuel. You step into the gap between two patrol paths and one Ember walks past you into the smoke before either of you realizes what happened.

failureEffect.narrative: The fire does not reveal itself to those who have not studied it.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The courtyard offers no quarter.

stages.title: The Keep Interior
stages.description: The keep is hotter than the courtyard. The stone walls retain heat like a kiln. The Ash Brutes guard the stairs. The Flamekeepers work behind them. At the center of the keep, the old hearth still burns — wrong color, wrong sound.

successEffect.narrative: The hearth fire is the wrong color — not orange but deep copper, with a core that pulses like a heartbeat. Someone with command of cold and lightning recognizes this: the fire spirit is still here, still bound, still alive inside the original ritual. The binding did not break in the explosion. It held. Whatever is in that hearth has been awake and aware for five years.

failureEffect.narrative: The hearth burns wrong but you cannot say why. The heat is oppressive enough that you stop trying to work it out.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The hearth is a fire. You focus on the enemies between you and the stairs.

successEffect.narrative: The garrison roof is half-collapsed but climbable. You get above the main hall before the Brute knows where to look. It never gets the chance to find out.

failureEffect.narrative: The walls are too hot to touch. You'll fight through the hall.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The walls are too hot to touch. You'll fight through the hall.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: You reach into the hearth's binding with cold and lightning — the only thing that can interrupt a fire spirit's frequency. The copper flame turns white. Something in there hears you and reaches back.

stages.title: The Throne of Ash
stages.description: The tower's top floor has been cleared of everything except ash and the chair Ignis placed at its center. He is sitting in it. He looks like he has been expecting someone for a very long time and is not particularly glad the wait is over.

successEffect.narrative: Ignis raises a hand when you enter. The Brute beside him goes still. 'One question,' he says. 'Before we do this.' He listens to whatever you say. He does not answer. But the Brute does not move when the fight starts.

failureEffect.narrative: Ignis watches you come through the door and says nothing. He has already decided.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Throne of Ash is not a place for conversation.

stages.title: The Cooled Hearth
stages.description: When the binding frequency breaks, the fire spirit comes out of the hearth the way something comes out of a room it has been locked in for five years. It is not grateful. But it is present in a way Ignis and his Remnant have never been — aware, communicative, furious with precision rather than madness. It attacks because you are the first thing it has reached that can be reached.

name: The Kennels of Wolf's Head
description: A Royal Guard war-dog breeding compound on the Vale's northern edge. The garrison was recalled five years ago. The dogs were not secured. The keepers who stayed behind have made arrangements.

lore.text: The compound was built to produce war dogs for the northern border. The dogs were exceptionally well trained. The recall order did not mention what to do with them. The garrison assumed someone else would handle it.

The Mad Keepers are not wrong that the pack follows them. What they are wrong about is why. The pack follows them because they know where the food stores are and because old habits persist even in feral animals. It is not loyalty. It is convenience. The Keepers have not noticed the difference.

The Alpha has been here longer than any of the Keepers. It was the garrison's original breeding sire — a controlled animal that the garrison used to keep the pack in line. When the garrison left it did what any reasonable apex predator would do with a suddenly unguarded territory. The Keepers think they manage it. They do not manage it.

The recall order came from the capital with one unusual addendum: the war dogs were not to be destroyed, relocated, or rehomed. They were to be left in place. The order was signed by a senior officer whose name has since been removed from the Royal Guard roster. Whatever the garrison was breeding here, someone in the capital wanted it to remain accessible.

stages.title: The Outer Pens
stages.description: The pens are open — the latches rusted through years ago. The Runts use the pen walls as cover and chase lines. The Mad Keepers whistle to coordinate them. The whistles are Royal Guard standard issue.

successEffect.narrative: The Royal Guard insignia carries a scent — or a memory of one. Two of the Runts stop at the edge of the pen and do not advance. They remember something about that smell, even if they cannot say what. The Mad Keeper nearest them does not override it. He remembers too.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing the pack recognizes. The Runts come out of the pens.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The compound does not distinguish between visitors.

successEffect.narrative: Fire is the one thing war dogs are trained to respect absolutely. You light something at the pen's edge before the Runts reach you. One cluster breaks off and does not return. The Mad Keeper nearest them starts backing up too.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no fire. The Runts read you correctly.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The pack does not hesitate for those it does not fear.

stages.title: The Central Yard
stages.description: The yard is where the garrison ran training exercises. The Blooded Hunters know this space the way a soldier knows a drill ground. The Kennel Master watches from the tower steps. He is the one with the secondary whistle.

successEffect.narrative: The Blooded Hunters are moving in the old training pattern — two-flank, one-center, rotating. Thirty years of conditioning survives five years of feral life. More than that: when the Alpha moves in the tower's shadow, the entire pack orients toward it. Not the Kennel Master. The Alpha. You have found the actual chain of command.

failureEffect.narrative: The pack moves too fast to read. You focus on the immediate threat.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Hunters do not pause to be observed.

successEffect.narrative: The northern compound wall is lower where the old kennel extension was built against it. You're over before the Blooded Hunters complete their circuit. One of them is still on the far side of the yard when the fight starts.

failureEffect.narrative: The compound walls are watched on all sides. You'll have to go through the gate.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The compound walls are watched on all sides. You'll have to go through the gate.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: You know where the Alpha is. You know it still responds to the old frequency. The Kennel Master's secondary whistle is hanging on the tower door. You take it before anyone stops you.

stages.title: The Command Tower
stages.description: The Alpha waits at the tower's base where the garrison commander used to stand during inspections. The Kennel Master is already there. They are, in some sense, holding court.

successEffect.narrative: You look directly at the Alpha and do not look away. It reads this correctly — not aggression, not submission. Something it was trained to recognize as authority. The Kennel Master steps back. The Alpha has already made its assessment and the Kennel Master has learned, in five years, to trust that assessment.

failureEffect.narrative: The Alpha looks at you and does not see what it is looking for. The Kennel Master advances.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The tower is the Alpha's territory. There is no approach it does not own.

stages.title: The Alpha's Truce
stages.description: The whistle brings the Alpha to the yard without the Kennel Master. Without the handlers. Just the Alpha and whatever is left of thirty years of training meeting whatever is left of five years of feral authority. It does not come peacefully. But it comes alone, which means you have answered one question: who is actually in charge here.

name: The Sealed Excavation
description: A collapsed mining shaft at the base of Stone Tooth Pass cliffs where Goldcrown Usurpers breached a Verdant Word ward ten years ago. They are still extracting crystals from the breach. The ward spirits are still trying to stop them. Neither side has made meaningful progress.

lore.text: The ward was placed here two hundred years before the succession crisis. The Order of the Verdant Word sealed something into the rock and built protections around it. The Goldcrown Usurpers found the site in a survey map and assumed the protections meant something valuable was inside. They were not wrong.

Foreman Kael has sent seventeen progress reports to Goldcrown command over ten years. He has received four responses. The last one, three years ago, instructed him to continue operations and not discuss the crystal growth with outside parties. He has not discussed it. The crystal growth has continued.

The Ward-Breakers knew the ward existed before they arrived. That information came from somewhere. The Verdant Word's site surveys were sealed with the dissolution of the Order — sealed meaning locked in the capital archive, which the Goldcrown command has been using as their administrative headquarters since the succession crisis began.

The Ward-Core is not a spirit. It is a memory — specifically, the accumulated memory of every Verdant Word blessing ever performed in the Silver Vale, stored in the stone because stone keeps things better than people do. The Order did not seal it here. They built here because it was already present. Whatever is growing in the crystal breach is not corruption. It is the memory trying to reconstitute itself.

The ward placed here two hundred years ago used a binding technique the Order learned from the Silverbough Elders. The elves called it a memory seal — not a barrier but a record, designed to store accumulated magical resonance against a time when it would be needed. The Order used it to contain a dangerous site. The elves intended it to be found by someone who could read what it had recorded. Two hundred years of accumulated memory is a great deal of information. The Ward-Core has been waiting for someone who could hear it.

stages.title: The Upper Tunnels
stages.description: The shaft entrance has been reinforced with dwarven timber and lit with lanterns that burn the wrong color — crystal light, not flame. The Delvers work in pairs. The Ward-Breakers move alone. Neither acknowledges the other if they can avoid it.

successEffect.narrative: The Loyalist seal creates a moment of confusion at the shaft entrance — the Delvers know the Goldcrown command sometimes sends inspectors with Loyalist credentials to check for contraband dealings. One pair breaks off to find the Foreman. They do not return in time.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credentials the excavation recognizes. The Delvers reach for their picks.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The shaft entrance is watched and there is no credential that opens it quietly.

successEffect.narrative: The ward is not broken — it is bent. The damage the Ward-Breakers have done has not severed the seal, only distorted it. Someone with nature magic can feel the original ward frequency still running underneath the breach, patient and persistent. The crystal formations near the entrance pulse in response. The Ward-Breakers nearest them go still, uncertain.

failureEffect.narrative: The ward energy in the tunnel is too distorted to read. You press on.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The tunnels give nothing away to those without the language to ask.

stages.title: The Breach Chamber
stages.description: The breach opens into a chamber that should not exist — too large for the shaft, walls lined with crystal that grows visibly between visits. The Captured Constructs labor at the crystal face. The Crystal Sentinels watch them from the ceiling, waiting for a moment of control-rod failure that has not come yet.

successEffect.narrative: The crystal formations respond to nature magic the way tuning forks respond to the right pitch. At the chamber's deepest point, behind the Constructs, the breach shows its actual shape — not a wound but a door, and something on the other side has noticed you. The Crystal Sentinels turn toward you at exactly the same moment, but their posture is different from when they faced the Delvers. They are assessing, not attacking.

failureEffect.narrative: The chamber is incomprehensible without the right frame of reference. You engage.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The breach offers nothing to those who cannot speak its frequency.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The door in the breach opens inward when nature magic touches it. The Ward-Core has been waiting for someone to knock correctly for two hundred years. You have knocked correctly.

stages.title: The Foreman's Office
stages.description: Kael has converted the deepest accessible chamber into his office. The crystal growth covers one entire wall. He works facing away from it. He knows it is there. He does not look at it while anyone else is present.

successEffect.narrative: You look directly at the crystal growth on Kael's arm and ask him about it before he can redirect you. He stops. The Ward-Breaker beside him looks away. Whatever Kael says next, he says it quietly, and the Ward-Breaker finds something urgent to attend to in the tunnel.

failureEffect.narrative: Kael reads you as an opponent and skips whatever he might have said. The Ward-Breakers advance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The office is not a place for conversation between strangers.

successEffect.narrative: Foreman Kael keeps a locked supply cache off the main shaft — rations, bandages, a sealed flare. Three years of careful rationing, undone in thirty seconds. The party enters the shaft better-supplied than Kael expected anyone to be.

failureEffect.narrative: The cache is locked and the Foreman is already moving. No time.

fallbackEffect.narrative: There's no cache visible from here. You advance on the shaft.

stages.title: The Ward Core
stages.description: The chamber beyond the door is not underground. It has no ceiling that you can see. The crystal formations here are different — organized rather than chaotic, arranged in patterns that the Verdant Word used in their oldest blessing diagrams. The Ward-Core is here. It has been here for longer than the Order existed. It is not pleased with recent events, and it does not distinguish between Goldcrown miners and uninvited visitors.

name: The Guildhall of Silent Trade
description: The Merchant Guild locked the Guildhall four years ago when the Royal Guard withdrew and the Verdant Word dissolved. The wealth inside is real. So is the starvation outside. The Guild considers this a negotiating position.

lore.text: The Guildhall has been sealed for four years. The Verdant Auditors have been petitioning for access for three. The Guild's official response to every petition is the same: 'When the roads are safe, trade will resume.' The Guild controls the only organization that could make the roads safe. They have not done this.
Guildmaster Vane's son held a Royal Guard commission that the Guild funded. The commission was purchased two years before the garrison recall. The timing is not a coincidence.

The Verdant Auditors in the outer courtyard were sent by the last functioning chapter of the Order's administrative body. They have legal authority to seize hoarded goods during a declared shortage. The shortage has been declared. The Guild has not recognized the declaration. The declaration was made by the same administrative body. The Guild does not recognize that either.

The Guild knew the Verdant Word would dissolve six months before it happened. The advance warning came through a Royal Guard officer — Vane's son, relaying intelligence from the garrison's courier network. The Guild used that window to consolidate assets, call in debts, and lock the gates. By the time the Order fell, the Guild was already positioned. The vault does not contain trade goods. It contains evidence.

stages.title: The Outer Courtyard
stages.description: The courtyard is a standoff. The Debt Collectors push the Verdant Auditors back from the gates. The Auditors push back with whatever legal authority they have left. Both sides are exhausted and neither has won in four years.

successEffect.narrative: The ledger has the Guildmaster's name in it — not directly, but the debt records point to a commission purchase that only the Guild could have funded. One Debt Collector reads the relevant page and steps back. He is not paid enough for this.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no leverage the Guild recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The courtyard dispute does not pause for visitors.

successEffect.narrative: The lead Auditor reads you as someone who understands what the Guild has done. She is not subtle about whose side she would prefer you to take. She shares what she knows about the vault's layout before the fighting starts.

failureEffect.narrative: The Auditor eyes you with the suspicion she reserves for anyone who might be Guild-connected.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The courtyard offers no allies to those without the right credentials.

successEffect.narrative: The service entrance on the north face hasn't been reinforced — guild lock, three pins, older model. You're inside before the Debt Collectors have finished their rotation. One of them spends the whole fight wondering how you got past the courtyard.

failureEffect.narrative: The Guildhall's main gate is the only way in. The Debt Collectors are watching it.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Guildhall's main gate is the only way in. The Debt Collectors are watching it.

stages.title: The Trading Floor
stages.description: The floor where the Vale's commerce used to move. The crates are still here. The silk is rotting. The Vault Wardens stand in formation around the inner gates. The Auditors who made it this far are trying to count inventory in the middle of a fight.

successEffect.narrative: The trading records stacked near the sealed inner door are open to the last active entries — four years ago, the week before the gates closed. The final transactions all move assets inward. Nothing moves outward. Whoever made these decisions knew the crisis was coming before it arrived.

failureEffect.narrative: The records are dense and the Vault Wardens are moving.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The trading floor keeps its own counsel.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The ledger opens a side door the Vault Wardens assumed was sealed. Someone on the inside left it coded to Vane family credentials. It leads down.

stages.title: The Vault Door
stages.description: Guildmaster Vane has turned the vault door into a command post. The paperwork is immaculate. The ledgers are current. He has been running the Guild from inside a locked building for four years and considers this a reasonable response to an unreasonable world.

successEffect.narrative: You name his son before the fight starts. Vane goes very still. The Vault Warden nearest him looks between you and his employer and makes a professional decision about how much he is paid to be part of this conversation.

failureEffect.narrative: Vane looks at you with the expression of someone who has been preparing for this conversation and is ready to have it badly.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The vault door is the end of the line.

successEffect.narrative: The unpaid ledger has Vane's name in Krog's handwriting. You present it alongside the Guild Seal and name the routing that connects them — the Goldcrown contract, the deliberate default, the Guild's advance knowledge, Vane's signature on the payment suppression order. Vane goes still for a long moment. The Vault Warden beside him reads the room and finds something pressing to attend to elsewhere.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the ledger but cannot present the case cleanly enough to matter. Vane has heard five years of accusations. He knows how to wait them out.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without the Stone-Tusk's annotated contract, there is no case to make — only allegations.

stages.title: The Arbitration Chamber
stages.description: Below the vault, through the door the ledger opened, there is a room that does not appear in the official Guild records. The evidence here is not trading records. It is correspondence — between the Guild, a Royal Guard officer, and three members of the Verdant Word administrative council. The dates are six months before the dissolution. Guildmaster Vane is already here. He has been expecting someone to find this room eventually. He is not surprised it took this long.

name: The Shrine of the First Oath
description: The island shrine where the halfling elders first signed the pact with Lyra three hundred years ago. The runes are fading. The spirits maintaining them have stopped understanding the difference between repair and harm.

lore.text: The pact was specific. Song and silver, seasonally, in exchange for safe passage. It was not a metaphor. The halfling communities came here to sing every equinox for two hundred years. They stopped when Bram died. The spirits noticed immediately.

The runes are not fading from neglect. They are being actively maintained by the Oath Wardens — incorrectly. The spirits remember the form of the ritual but not the substance. They are writing the right words in the wrong medium. They have been doing this for thirty years.

High Keeper Elara signed the original agreement. She was old when the halfling elders came to the shrine. She is older now. She remembers Lyra's expression when the pact was sealed — not relief, not satisfaction. Something more complicated than either. She has been trying to understand what that expression meant for three hundred years.

Lyra was never bound by force. The halflings did not trap her — they asked her, and she agreed, and the agreement was freely given because she wanted the company. The pact was not a cage. It was a relationship. What is breaking now is not a magical contract. It is thirty years of silence from people she loved.

stages.title: The Island Approach
stages.description: The Silverstream runs slow and red near the standing stones. The Rune Scrubbers work at the stone faces with tools that are entirely wrong for the job. The Oath Wardens watch the bank.

successEffect.narrative: You place a halfling silver coin in the water before crossing. The Rune Scrubbers nearest the bank go still. Two of them turn back to the stones. This is not the song, but it is silver, and silver is half the pact, and half the pact is more than the shrine has received in thirty years.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no silver to offer. The Scrubbers do not pause.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The shrine does not acknowledge those who arrive empty-handed.

successEffect.narrative: The rune carvings respond to nature magic — not the current botched maintenance, but something underneath it, the original frequency of the pact. You can feel the shape of what the agreement was supposed to be. The Oath Wardens register this response and hold their position, uncertain.

failureEffect.narrative: The runes are obscured by thirty years of incorrect maintenance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The standing stones are old stone and fading marks.

stages.title: The Shrine Interior
stages.description: Inside the stone circle, the rune work is densest and the damage is worst. The Pact Breakers move between the standing stones, applying corrections that undo the corrections before them. The Oath Wardens enforce the process without understanding it.

successEffect.narrative: The shrine's central stone is not empty. There is a presence inside it — very old, very tired, and aware of you in a way the Scrubbers and Wardens are not. Holy magic resonates with it. It does not communicate clearly, but you understand: it is trying to finish something, and it has been trying for a very long time, and it is glad someone finally came who might understand what it is trying to do.

failureEffect.narrative: The central stone is cold. You focus on the spirits between you and it.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The shrine's heart is sealed to those without the right understanding.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The central stone opens to holy magic. Elara reaches through and pulls the resonance toward you. The rune circle activates — not the broken version, but the original. The Pact Chamber is inside.

stages.title: The Rune Circle
stages.description: High Keeper Elara at the center of the standing stones, maintaining the runes with the method she has been using for thirty years. The method does not work. She knows this. She has not stopped.

successEffect.narrative: You tell Elara what the runes are supposed to say, not what she has been writing. She goes still. One of the Oath Wardens lowers its posture — this is the closest thing to surprise a three-hundred-year-old spirit can show.

failureEffect.narrative: Elara looks at you and sees another intruder. The Wardens advance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The rune circle does not pause for strangers.

stages.title: The Pact Chamber
stages.description: Inside the original rune circle, beneath the damaged surface work, the pact is intact. Elara has been maintaining this inner layer alone for thirty years while the outer layer collapsed. She has not told the other spirits. She did not want them to know how close the pact came to failing entirely. She fights now because you have entered something she has kept private for three centuries, and she is not sure you have the right to know what is in here.

name: The Ancestral Forge
description: The foundry where the Forge-Kings of Khaz-Morad crafted the ancestral weapons and bound the spirits of master smiths into the walls. Both dwarven factions broke the seals simultaneously four years ago. The forge's defenses woke up. Nobody has left.

lore.text: The seals required the kingdom to be united before anyone could enter. Both factions broke them on the same day because both factions had the same intelligence about where the armory was. That intelligence came from the same source. Neither faction has asked where the other got it.

The construct defenses were not built to distinguish between Ironvein and Goldcrown. They were built to distinguish between authorized and unauthorized. The oaths required for authorization specified a united kingdom. Neither faction qualifies. Both are equally unwelcome.

The soldiers trapped inside have formed a truce. They share supplies, rotate watches, and do not discuss the war. Two Ironvein soldiers and one Goldcrown soldier have been sharing a sleeping chamber for three years. They do not know each other's names. They have decided this is better.

The ancestors did not choose either faction. The forge's central record, sealed behind the King's Bench, contains the Foreman's assessment of both claimants — made when the seals broke and the Foreman reviewed the credentials of everyone who entered. The assessment is brief. It names a third candidate, a minor line that both factions overlooked because the line had no army. It has the right blood.

stages.title: The Outer Corridors
stages.description: The corridor sweeps happen every four hours. The trapped soldiers have learned the timing. The constructs have not learned that the soldiers know. Three years of this and neither side has adapted.

successEffect.narrative: The Loyalist seal creates a moment of professional confusion — the Goldcrown soldier recognizes it as enemy credentials but also as proof you are not a construct. One of them decides this is someone else's problem and backs into a side passage.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credentials either faction recognizes. Both advance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The forge does not distinguish between kinds of intruder.

successEffect.narrative: The rune patterns on the corridor walls are maintenance logs — every weapon forged, every smith who worked here, every oath sworn over the anvil. A character who understands physical craft reads them correctly: this place was built to last, and it was built with standards that neither faction currently meets.

failureEffect.narrative: The runes are dense technical notation. You move on.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The corridors keep their records from those who cannot read them.

stages.title: The Armory Hall
stages.description: The armory itself. Weapons the war has been fought over for four years, untouched because the constructs guard them and the soldiers guard each other. The Stone Wardens patrol in formation. The trapped veterans watch from alcoves.

successEffect.narrative: The signet ring resonates with something in the far wall — a sealed chamber behind the main armory that the constructs ignore on their sweeps. The Ironvein soldiers see it and go very still. None of them have gotten this close to the inner seals. They are not sure if what they feel is hope or fear.

failureEffect.narrative: The armory walls show nothing to those without the ancestral mark.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The armory is sealed stone and old runes.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The signet opens the King's Bench. The Foreman's seal breaks. The assessment inside has been waiting four hundred years for someone with the right credentials to read it.

stages.title: The Central Anvil Chamber
stages.description: The Awakened Foreman holds the center of the forge. He has reviewed the credentials of everyone who has entered in four years. His assessment of the quality of the current claimants is visible in how he moves when he sees them.

successEffect.narrative: The Foreman watches you approach and does not immediately advance. He is evaluating. A party that meets his standard — not by faction, not by blood, by demonstrated conviction — gets a pause. One of the Stone Wardens steps back. This is not approval. It is assessment suspended pending further evidence.

failureEffect.narrative: The Foreman advances without hesitation. You do not meet his standard.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The central chamber does not pause for those who enter without authorization.

stages.title: The King's Bench
stages.description: The assessment chamber. The Foreman's written record of every candidate who has entered since the seals broke — including the one candidate neither faction considered, the one whose credentials the signet ring matches. The Foreman is here too. He fights because the record is not for general reading. It is for one specific person, and you are not that person yet.

name: The Watchtower of the Northern Lights
description: A joint Ironvein-Goldcrown monitoring post above the Sunken Sanctum crater lake. Five years ago the Tidebound Penitents came up from the water. The dwarves who were here sent a warning to the Capital. The Capital did not respond. The warning was accurate.

lore.text: The monitoring post was the only joint operation the two dwarven factions ever agreed on. They agreed because both sides were frightened of the same thing. Shared fear turned out to be a better foundation for cooperation than shared interest.

Watch Commander Thrain sent three warnings to the Capital. The first two were acknowledged. The third was not. The third was the one that said the post had been compromised and the Tidebound Penitents were moving south. That warning was sent five years ago.

The Capital's archive contains Thrain's three reports, filed and catalogued. They are stored in the same section as the Verdant Auditors' petitions and the garrison recall orders. Someone filed them correctly. Someone read them and decided not to act. Those are the same person.

The monitoring crystal in the tower's core was calibrated to the water spirit's resonance frequency. It has been recording continuously for ten years. The recordings show the spirit's pattern changing — not expanding randomly, but deliberately, in a specific direction, toward a specific point. Thrain understood what the pattern meant. His final report named the destination. The Capital filed it.

stages.title: The Frozen Approach
stages.description: The frost starts a hundred yards from the tower and gets thicker as you approach. The Frostbite Conscripts patrol in the old dwarven rotation patterns. They are very thorough. They have been very thorough for five years.

successEffect.narrative: The Verdant Word Seal resonates with something in the tower's monitoring array — the ward frequency the Order used to track magical disturbances. The Tidebound cannot interfere with that frequency without alerting the array. One Conscript's patrol pattern stutters. The gap is small and temporary.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing that disrupts the patrol coverage.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The frozen approach has no quiet side.

successEffect.narrative: Fire is the one thing the Tidebound cannot maintain in the presence of. A controlled application drives one Tide Warden back from the approach path. The frost recedes slightly where you stand. The Conscripts do not react — fire is outside their patrol parameters.

failureEffect.narrative: The cold is absolute here. You have nothing that reaches it.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The approach offers no advantage to those without fire.

successEffect.narrative: The Verdant Word Seal and the Oath Stone broadcast simultaneously — the ward frequency and the pact frequency running together create an interference pattern the Tidebound's curse cannot cleanly operate through. Two Frostbite Conscripts stagger as the signal governing their patrol pattern stutters. The gap closes in seconds but it is long enough.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the seal but the second frequency is missing. The disruption is partial and the Conscripts compensate.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without the ward frequency, the approach offers no disruption.

successEffect.narrative: You come up the crater rim rather than the main path — steeper, but unguarded. You reach the tower with the approach behind you and the Penitents ahead. The altitude clears your head.

failureEffect.narrative: The main path is the only way up. The Penitents are already on it.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The main path is the only way up. The Penitents are already on it.

stages.title: The Tower Interior
stages.description: The monitoring equipment is still running. The Storm Callers have grown around it without understanding what it does. The Tide Wardens ignore it. The crystal array records everything, including this visit.

successEffect.narrative: Cold magic resonates with the crystal array's recording medium — the same elemental signature the crystals were calibrated to track. A character with cold magic can read the recordings directly. What the crystal has been tracking for ten years is not diffuse expansion. It is a vector. The water spirit is moving toward something specific, and the direction has not changed.

failureEffect.narrative: The crystal array runs but you cannot read the recordings.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The monitoring equipment operates in a frequency you cannot access.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The Verdant Word Seal activates the tower's original monitoring protocol. Thrain's final report loads automatically. The Monitoring Chamber opens.

stages.title: The Observation Deck
stages.description: Watch Commander Thrain at the tower's highest point, watching the crater lake below. He has been watching it for five years. His understanding of the water spirit's movements is more accurate than anything in the Capital's archive. He is going to fight you because that is what he does now.

successEffect.narrative: You ask Thrain about his final report before the fight starts. He turns from the lake. Whatever you say acknowledges that you know about the warning he sent and understand what it meant. One Storm Caller dissipates — Thrain's genuine attention is enough to disrupt a construct built from his residual alertness.

failureEffect.narrative: Thrain does not turn from the lake. The Storm Callers advance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The observation deck offers no conversation.

stages.title: The Monitoring Chamber
stages.description: The chamber where the crystal array's full output is stored. Thrain's final report is here — not the filed summary the Capital received, but the complete version with the destination named. The water spirit is not expanding toward the Vale. It is moving toward the Spire of Silent Conviction. Thrain understood why. He included the reason in the report. The Capital received the summary. The reason was in the section that was not summarized.

name: The Salt-Wept Beacon
description: A lighthouse at the mouth of the Silverstream where it meets the Northern Sea, maintained for centuries as a shrine to Lyra. Five years ago the fishing village at its base performed a forbidden ritual to bind Lyra's power to their nets. The catches improved. The light changed. Ships followed it onto the rocks.

lore.text: The fishing village had three bad seasons before the elder proposed the ritual. He had found a fragment of the old halfling binding ceremony in a water-damaged Order archive. He performed the parts he could read. The parts he could not read he improvised. The catches improved the following week.

The ships that follow the beacon light do not survive the rocks. The Salt-Wept believe they are guiding the ships safely. They have been guiding ships onto rocks for five years and have not connected the belief to the outcome. This is not stupidity. It is something worse.

The Keeper remembers the Shrine of the First Oath. Not because he visited it — he did not — but because the fragment of Lyra he merged with carries the memory of the original pact. He hums the halfling offering hymn without knowing what it is. He has been humming it for five years. The other Salt-Wept have learned it from him.

The fragment trapped in the lighthouse is not what the ritual pulled out of Lyra. It is what Lyra pushed out — deliberately, the moment she felt the binding begin. She severed the piece of herself most vulnerable to the ritual and let them take it, to protect the rest. The fragment has been trying to get back to her ever since. It fights because it cannot tell the difference between someone who wants to free it and someone who wants to bind it further.

stages.title: The Rocky Approach
stages.description: The shore below the lighthouse is littered with wreckage from ships the beacon has lured in. The Tide-Callers wade through the tidal pools. The Salt-Singers stand on the higher rocks and sing to the fog.

successEffect.narrative: You place a halfling coin in the water before stepping onto the rocks. One of the Tide-Callers stops moving. The coin is not enough — it is a fraction of what the pact required — but the Salt-Wept recognize the shape of it. One of them turns back. Not away from you. Toward the lighthouse. As if remembering something.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing the shore recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The rocks offer no quarter.

successEffect.narrative: The beacon light is cold at its core — not the cold of winter but the cold of deep water, the temperature of the place Lyra originally came from. A character with cold magic recognizes the frequency. The light is not corrupted uniformly. There is a pulse in it, irregular, like something trying to signal through interference.

failureEffect.narrative: The beacon light is wrong but you cannot say how.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The shore offers nothing to those who cannot read the water.

stages.title: The Lighthouse Interior
stages.description: The staircase is slick with salt and algae. The Fog-Weavers work in the lantern room above. The Salt-Singers patrol the middle levels. The light pulses irregularly — the signal in it more visible from inside.

successEffect.narrative: The irregular pulse in the beacon light is Lyra's original pact frequency — the same tone that resonates at the Shrine of the First Oath, the same pattern that runs through the silver halfling coins. A character with holy magic can isolate it from the corruption around it. What is in the light is trying to speak. It is not the Keeper's fragment. It is something older, and smaller, and very specifically directed at whoever is standing in this room right now.

failureEffect.narrative: The light pulses but carries nothing you can read.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The lantern room hides its signals from those without the right frequency.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The oath stone resonates with the pulse in the light. The fragment recognizes the pact frequency. A door in the lighthouse base that was sealed opens — not a physical door, a frequency door, a place in the light where you can reach through and touch what is inside.

stages.title: The Lantern Room
stages.description: The Keeper at the top of the lighthouse, maintaining the corrupted beacon by hand. The light is wrong because he cannot stop adjusting it. He is trying to make it right. He does not know what right looks like anymore.

successEffect.narrative: You sing the first verse of the halfling offering hymn — the real version, not the wrong one he has been humming. The Keeper goes absolutely still. The Fog-Weaver nearest him loses coherence and disperses. The Keeper does not attack immediately. He is listening.

failureEffect.narrative: You do not know the hymn. The Keeper does not pause.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The lantern room does not reward those who arrive without the right words.

stages.title: The Frequency Door
stages.description: The fragment of Lyra that has been trapped in the light for five years. Smaller than a spirit, larger than an echo. It fights because fighting is what it has been doing since the moment it was separated — not from malice but from the accumulated momentum of five years of trying to get back to where it came from. When it is done fighting, if it can be brought to that point, it will be done with the lighthouse entirely.

name: The Whispering Athenaeum
description: The Verdant Word's primary archive, housed inside a hollow sequoia deep in the Whispering Woods. Fifty years ago a preservation ritual used blood-contaminated ink and the scribes died in their seats. They are still working.

lore.text: The preservation ritual was intended to make the texts permanent. It worked. The texts will last indefinitely. So will the scribes who were writing them when the ink made contact. The Order considered this an acceptable outcome for seventeen minutes before reversing that assessment.

The Grand Archivist began removing texts from the official catalog six months before the dissolution. The removals were authorized at the highest level. The authorization came from the High Council directly, not through the standard administrative process. He asked why once. He was told the texts were sensitive. He did not ask again.

The Inkbound Scribes do not know the Order dissolved. The cursed ink carries the last instructions they received — preserve, protect, deny access. They have been following those instructions for fifty years. Nobody has come to give them new ones. Nobody who entered the archive after the dissolution left it.

The restricted archive contains the correspondence between the High Council and three entities whose names do not appear in any other Order record. One is a merchant. One is a Royal Guard officer. One is unnamed but identified by a sigil that appears in the Spire of Silent Conviction's sealed vault. The restricted texts were not removed to protect the Order. They were removed to protect these three from the Order.

The Athenaeum was built inside a sequoia because the Silverbough Elders specified it. Living wood holds knowledge differently than stone — the bark absorbs the resonance of what is written near it and the text becomes part of the tree's memory over time. The elves taught this to the Order's first archivists. The texts the Grand Archivist removed from the catalog six months before the dissolution were specifically the ones that documented that teaching — the ones that would tell anyone who found them that the Gallery of Fractured Light exists and where.

stages.title: The Outer Stacks
stages.description: The archive entrance is still organized. The bark scrolls are shelved correctly. The Scriveners move through the stacks in patterns that would make sense if they were maintaining a working library. They are maintaining a working library. It is simply no longer accessible to the living.

successEffect.narrative: The Verdant Word Seal carries the Order's official authorization frequency. Two Scriveners recognize it and return to their shelves. They are not deferring to you. They are deferring to the credentials. There is a difference, and in this archive it matters.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credentials the archive recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The archive does not acknowledge unauthorized visitors.
