successEffect.narrative: The cursed ink has a shadow resonance — it was mixed with something from the other side of the visible spectrum, which is what makes it permanent and what makes it lethal. A character with shadow magic can read the ink directly, including the parts that have been written over. Beneath the official catalog entries there are deletion marks — texts that were removed before the dissolution, recorded as removed but not identified.

failureEffect.narrative: The ink is ink to those who cannot read its other layer.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The stacks reveal nothing to those without the right sight.

successEffect.narrative: Among the bark scrolls near the entrance there are texts written in a different hand — older than the Order's archivists, in a script that uses nature-frequency markers as punctuation. Elf work, teaching work, the kind of text written to be understood by students rather than preserved for posterity. The content is technical: how to bind nature spirits without breaking them, how to maintain a pact across generations, how to read the land's frequency for sites of power. This is what the Order learned from. The Scriveners walk past these texts without seeing them. The ink that took the scribes is human-formula. It cannot read elf script.

failureEffect.narrative: The outer stacks are bark scrolls and old wood.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The archive does not reveal itself to those without the right frequency.

stages.title: The Reading Room
stages.description: The inner reading room where the restricted texts were kept. The Silencers patrol here — not the general archive Silencers but ones specifically assigned to this section. The restriction predates the dissolution. The Silencers are enforcing an order that came from the High Council itself.

successEffect.narrative: The reading room's far wall has a section the ink has marked differently — not the standard preservation pattern but something that uses the ink's lethal properties deliberately, as a lock. Shadow magic reads the lock's structure. It is keyed to the Grand Archivist's name, which means it can be opened the same way the Archivist opens it — by knowing exactly what is inside and approaching it with that knowledge visible.

failureEffect.narrative: The far wall is shelving. You move on.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The reading room keeps its sealed section from those who cannot read the lock.

successEffect.narrative: The sealed section is locked with a standard Order mechanism — designed to keep out the uninitiated, not a thief. The shelves inside hold working notes from before the preservation ritual. One set of annotations, in a different hand from the rest, describes the blood-ink contamination as deliberate. The author's name has been removed. The handwriting is still there.

failureEffect.narrative: The sealed section's lock holds. Whatever is in there stays in there.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The sealed section is locked. The Silencers are already aware of you.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The lock opens to shadow magic applied with the right understanding. The sealed section is behind the official catalog. The Grand Archivist is already there.

stages.title: The Central Catalog
stages.description: The Grand Archivist at the heart of the sequoia, merged with the tree's root system and the catalog it contains. He knows every text in the archive including the ones he removed. He will not discuss the removals.

successEffect.narrative: You ask the Archivist not for the restricted texts but for the deletion records — the catalog entries that show something was removed, without asking what was removed. He pauses. This is a technically permissible request. He processes it. One Silencer disperses while he is deciding.

failureEffect.narrative: The Archivist does not engage with questions from unauthorized visitors.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The central catalog does not offer conversation.

stages.title: The Sealed Section
stages.description: The restricted archive. The True Record — the Grand Archivist's shadow-self, the part of him that knows what these texts contain and has been fighting with the part of him that is trying to protect them for fifty years. He fights now because you have reached the texts and the protection protocol has overridden the knowledge of why protection is wrong.

name: The Bastion of the Fractured Ward
description: A Royal Guard fortress on the Capital's outskirts where the Verdant Word was performing a protective ward when the order dissolved. The priests fled mid-ritual. The soldiers who were guarding them did not survive the fracture. The cultists who arrived afterward to exploit the unstable magic have not improved the situation.

lore.text: The ward was designed to protect the Capital from magical incursion. The priests abandoned it when the dissolution order came. The soldiers guarding them had no way to know the dissolution order was coming. The soldiers did not survive the fracture. The priests did. This fact is not recorded in any official document.

The Ward-Breaker cultists arrived within a week of the fracture. They knew the ward would fracture before it happened. The only people who knew the dissolution was coming in advance were the High Council and three entities whose names appear in the Athenaeum's restricted archive.

The Ward Captain has held the Bastion for fifty years with the same order he received on the day of the fracture. The order was to protect the Capital from magical threat. He has interpreted this to include the Ward-Breaker cultists, the fractured ward itself, and anyone who enters the Bastion without authorization. His authorization list has not been updated since the dissolution.

The fractured ward is not destroying itself. It is trying to complete the original ritual without the priests. It has been trying for fifty years. What it has achieved is a half-formed purification effect that neither succeeds nor fails — it simply continues, pulling magical energy from everything in range. The Ward Captain understands this. He has been using himself as fuel for the ward's completion attempt for fifty years. He intends to continue until it finishes or he runs out.

stages.title: The Outer Battlements
stages.description: The Bastion's outer walls are intact. The Ghost Conscripts patrol them in the patterns they were trained for. The Ward-Breaker cultists move through gaps in the patrol like they know the timing. They have had fifty years to learn it.

successEffect.narrative: The Royal Guard insignia carries the authorization frequency the conscripts were trained to recognize. Two Ghost Conscripts step aside. They are not letting you past — they are deferring to a credential they do not have the authority to reject. The Ward-Breakers watch this with visible frustration.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credentials the Bastion recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The outer walls do not open for unauthorized visitors.

successEffect.narrative: The fractured ward still has the shape of its original purpose — a Verdant Word purification ritual, half-complete, pulling inward rather than radiating outward. Nature magic reads the original intent clearly. The ward is not broken. It is suspended. The difference matters for what comes next.

failureEffect.narrative: The ward energy is chaotic and unreadable.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The fracture offers nothing to those without the right frame of reference.

successEffect.narrative: The armory side gate uses a Royal Guard standard lock — the kind issued to every garrison in the Vale before the dissolution. One of the Ward-Breakers is covering the main entrance. The armory entrance is not the main entrance.

failureEffect.narrative: The fortress gates are sealed with ward-fractured iron. Nothing short of the right authorization opens them cleanly.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The fortress gates are sealed with ward-fractured iron. Nothing short of the right authorization opens them cleanly.

stages.title: The Central Courtyard
stages.description: The courtyard where the ward fracture is most visible. The wardsigils in the stone are still active, still pulling. The Spectral Veterans hold the perimeter. The Ward-Breaker cultists work at the sigils with tools that are extracting something they do not fully understand.

successEffect.narrative: The suspended ritual has a center — a point in the courtyard where the original purification effect is strongest. Nature magic reaches it directly. What is there is not a threat. It is an incompletion, and it is aware of being incomplete, and it is aware of you as someone who might be able to complete it or at least understand why it cannot.

failureEffect.narrative: The wardsigils are damaged beyond reading.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The courtyard offers nothing to those without nature magic.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The ward recognizes nature magic as the frequency it was designed for. The fractured half-ritual responds. A passage opens into the ward's core. The Ward Captain is already there.

stages.title: The Ward Core Chamber
stages.description: The keep's interior where the fractured ward is most concentrated. The Ward Captain at the center, channeling the ward's pull through himself. He has been doing this for fifty years. He intends to continue.

successEffect.narrative: You tell the Ward Captain his orders were fulfilled — the Capital is still standing. He turns. He considers this. One Spectral Veteran disperses. The Captain has not dismissed you. He is deciding whether fulfilled means the same as complete.

failureEffect.narrative: The Ward Captain does not engage with arguments from unauthorized visitors.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The ward core does not pause.

stages.title: The Ward's Core
stages.description: What the ward has been trying to complete for fifty years. It is not a threat. It is the original purification ritual, held in suspension because it never finished. It fights because the ritual's design treats unresolved magical energy as a threat to be purified — and a party that can reach this room carries a great deal of unresolved magical energy.

name: The Marsh of Broken Oaths
description: The wetland where the Verdant Word first attempted the water-spirit binding three hundred years ago. The attempt failed. The corruption from that failure is still here. The Tidebound Vanguard moved in five years ago to use it as a staging ground. The three remaining Sealkeepers of the Verdant Word are still trying to contain it.

lore.text: The binding failed here three hundred years ago. The failure left a residue — not hostile, not benign, simply present, a scar in the land's magical substrate. The Order built monitoring wards around it and left it alone. When the Order dissolved, the monitoring wards stopped being maintained. The residue started spreading.

The Tidebound Vanguard chose this location specifically. Not because it is strategically optimal for a southern advance — it is not. Because the corrupted residue here responds to the water spirit's frequency. The marsh is not a staging ground. It is an amplifier.

The three Sealkeepers have names: Aldric, Mira, and Conn. They have been in the marsh for five years fighting a losing battle with limited supplies and no support from what remains of the Order's administrative body. The administrative body knows they are here. It has not sent supplies because it no longer has supplies to send. The Sealkeepers know this. They are still here.

The original binding failure was not an accident. The Verdant Word's records — the restricted ones in the Athenaeum's sealed section — describe the first attempt as deliberately sabotaged. Not by an enemy. By one of the three entities whose names appear in the restricted archive. The marsh's scar is not a failed ritual. It is the evidence of the first act in a fifty-year plan to bring the water spirit south.

The Silverbough Elders refused to participate in the original binding attempt. The Verdant Word asked them directly — this was before the withdrawal, when the relationship was still functional — and the elves declined without explanation. The Order interpreted this as territorial disagreement. It was not. The elves had already seen the marsh's ley line and recognized that it had been redirected. They knew the attempt would fail. They did not say why they knew.

stages.title: The Outer Reeds
stages.description: The marsh edge is where the Drowned Scouts patrol. The water glows faintly — the amplifier effect is already active. The Sealkeepers have planted ward-stakes along the eastern bank. The Tidebound have been pulling them out for five years.

successEffect.narrative: The oath stone's pact frequency disrupts the amplifier effect in the immediate vicinity. Two Drowned Scouts lose coherence briefly — the water spirit's signal they are channeling stutters. They pull back into the deeper water. The Mist Weaver watches this with visible concern.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing that disrupts the amplifier.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The marsh does not yield ground to those without the pact frequency.

successEffect.narrative: The ward-stakes the Sealkeepers planted use a nature-magic frequency you can read directly. Following it leads you to the eastern bank where Aldric is watching the water. He does not lower his weapon when he sees you. He does lower it slightly. He has not seen a friendly face in five years.

failureEffect.narrative: The marsh is too loud to find anything small and quiet.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The reeds keep their secrets from those who cannot read the ward frequency.

stages.title: The Camp Perimeter
stages.description: The Tidebound's forward camp on a rise of solid ground in the marsh's center. The Barnacle Brutes anchor the perimeter. The Mist Weavers work the fog between. The Sealkeepers have been unable to reach this far. Nobody has told the Tidebound that three people have been trying for five years.

successEffect.narrative: The ley line running through the camp's center is the amplifier's core — the original binding failure's scar, three hundred years of passive corruption, now actively channeling the water spirit's frequency toward the south. Nature magic reads the original ley line beneath the corruption. It is not destroyed. It is redirected. Someone redirected it deliberately, a long time ago, before the Tidebound arrived. The corruption predates the Tidebound by two hundred and fifty years.

failureEffect.narrative: The ley line is too corrupted to read.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The camp offers nothing to those without nature magic.

successEffect.narrative: You present the Verdant Word Seal and the Oath Stone together — Order authority and pact evidence in the same hands. Aldric sees them both and calls to the other Sealkeepers. The Barnacle Brute nearest the ward-stakes hesitates; the Tidebound signal that governs it conflicts with something the dual credentials are broadcasting. The hesitation is brief but it is real.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the credentials but the presentation does not land with the force the moment requires.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without the Order seal, the pact stone alone is not enough to shift the Tidebound's advance.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The Athenaeum scroll maps the ley line's original configuration. The corruption was introduced at a specific point — a ritual site thirty yards north of the camp that the Tidebound are using as a water source without knowing what it is. The Sealkeeper Aldric sees the scroll and immediately understands. He leads you there.

stages.title: The Ley Line Altar
stages.description: High Chanter Malacor at the altar where the ley line was originally corrupted. He knows what this place is. He has been here for five years maintaining the corruption deliberately. He does not know who introduced it two hundred and fifty years ago. He does not care. It serves the same purpose.

successEffect.narrative: You ask Malacor who corrupted the ley line originally. He stops. He has not been asked this before. He does not know the answer. The Barnacle Brute nearest him loses orientation briefly — Malacor's attention is the signal it follows, and Malacor is no longer paying attention to the fight.

failureEffect.narrative: Malacor does not engage with questions from intruders.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The altar does not pause.

stages.title: The Original Corruption
stages.description: The ritual site where the ley line was first redirected. The Sealkeeper Aldric stands at its edge and does not go further. Whatever is here is three hundred years old and he does not have the tools to address it. You do. The Athenaeum scroll maps the original corruption point exactly. The entity that performed the original corruption left a signature. The signature matches the sigil from the Spire of Silent Conviction's sealed vault.

name: The Spire of Silent Conviction
description: The conclave seat of the Verdant Word's High Council, sealed by the Capital's mages fifty years ago after the Council disappeared mid-session. The seal has held. The echoes of what the Council was debating when the dissolution happened have not.

lore.text: The Capital sealed the Spire the day after the dissolution. The official reason given was magical contamination. The actual reason — the Council was mid-vote on whether to expose three entities who had infiltrated the Order — appears in no official document. It appears in the Athenaeum's restricted archive, in the monitoring crystal's final recording, and in Thrain's complete report. The Capital has all three. They were filed correctly.

The High Speaker Valerius lost his voice at the moment of dissolution. He has been in the Spire for fifty years with nothing to say and everything to say. The Echo Council exists because the debate that was interrupted has to be completed by someone. They cannot complete it themselves. The vote was three to two in favor of exposure. The dissolution happened before the result could be enacted. The three entities know this. They have had fifty years to ensure the result stays unenacted.

The three entities whose names appear in the restricted archive are: a merchant named Vane, a Royal Guard officer whose commission was purchased by that merchant, and the unnamed third — identified only by a sigil that appears in the sealed vault. The Spire is sealed because opening it completes the vote. The vote's completion would make the entities' names public record. The Capital sealed it to prevent this. The Capital was asked to seal it. The question is who asked.

The Unfinished Debate is not a spirit. It is the vote itself — three to two, sitting in the sealed vault for fifty years, waiting to be cast. The unnamed third entity is not a person. It is the water spirit. The original binding failure in the marsh was not an accident. It was the water spirit's first attempt to reach the Capital. The Verdant Word found out. The Council voted to expose it. The dissolution was the water spirit's response. It has been completing its approach for fifty years. The Spire is the last thing standing between the vote and the outcome.

The Silverbough Elves sealed the Gallery bridges four days after the dissolution. The dissolution happened on a Tuesday. The bridges were sealed on Saturday. The elves do not act on four days' notice unless they already knew what was coming. They had been watching the High Council's debate through the Ward-Core's memory network for months. They knew the vote would be three to two. They knew the dissolution would follow. They sealed the bridges before the world outside the Gallery understood that the Order was gone.

stages.title: The Silent Approach
stages.description: Sound dampens within a hundred yards of the Spire. The Silent Acolytes patrol the perimeter in absolute quiet. The sound-dampening is not a property of the building. It is a property of the debate that is still happening inside it.

successEffect.narrative: The Athenaeum scroll carries the High Council's seal. Two Silent Acolytes recognize it and step aside. They are not welcoming you. They are acknowledging that you carry evidence relevant to the unfinished debate. The debate's rules permit the introduction of new evidence. Two Acolytes step aside because the rules say they must.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credentials the Spire recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The approach does not open for the unauthorized.

successEffect.narrative: The silence is not absence. It is content — the accumulated weight of fifty years of an unfinished vote, pressing outward from the Spire's center. Holy magic reads silence as a kind of speech. What the Spire is saying, very quietly, is: the vote is three to two, and it needs a fifth voice to break the tie, and the tie has been broken the same way for fifty years, and it is not sustainable.

failureEffect.narrative: The silence is silence.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The approach offers nothing to those who cannot hear what is not said.

stages.title: The Council Chamber
stages.description: The chamber where the vote was taking place when the dissolution happened. The chairs are still arranged for a session. The Ward Keepers stand at the doors. The Muted Judges move between the chairs, maintaining a debate that has been ongoing for fifty years.

successEffect.narrative: The chamber's walls have the vote written into them — not visibly, but in the shadow layer that the cursed ink the Athenaeum used shares with this place. Three votes for exposure. Two votes against. The two against are signed with names that match the restricted archive entries. The unnamed third vote is not a Council member's signature. It is the water spirit's sigil. The dissolution happened in the moment between the third yes vote being cast and the result being recorded.

failureEffect.narrative: The chamber keeps its records.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The vote is sealed from those without the right sight.

successEffect.narrative: The Athenaeum scroll and the monitoring crystal read each other — the scroll carries the vote's written record, the crystal carries the recording of the chamber at the moment it was cast. Together they reconstruct the full sequence: the three yes votes, the two no votes, the water spirit's sigil appearing in the chamber's resonance field at the exact moment of the third yes. The Ward Keeper nearest the sealed vault registers this as authorized access. It steps aside. The Muted Judges do not.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the scroll but cannot align it with the chamber's recording medium precisely enough to reconstruct the sequence.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without the Athenaeum scroll, the monitoring crystal has no written record to correlate against.

successEffect.narrative: The archive lock is fifty years old and has not been maintained. Three pins, corroded. The door opens into a chamber the Ward-Keepers haven't entered since the sealing. The Council's final session notes are still on the table. The last entry is mid-sentence. Whatever interrupted it, the scribe did not consider it worth finishing the thought before whatever happened, happened.

failureEffect.narrative: The archive is sealed behind a ward-reinforced lock. The Ward-Keepers are already aware of you.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The archive is sealed behind a ward-reinforced lock. The Ward-Keepers are already aware of you.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The monitoring crystal resonates with the Spire's recording medium — the same frequency Thrain's watchtower used to track the water spirit. In this room, with this crystal, the unfinished vote becomes legible. The sealed vault opens. The Unfinished Debate surfaces.

stages.title: The Speaker's Podium
stages.description: High Speaker Valerius at the podium where he stood when the dissolution happened. He has not moved in fifty years. He fights because the debate protocol requires him to challenge anyone who approaches the podium without being recognized by the chair. He is the chair. He does not recognize you.

successEffect.narrative: You cast the fifth vote before the fight starts — aloud, in the chamber, in the form the debate protocol requires. Valerius goes still. The debate rules permit a new voice to break a tie. One Ward Keeper disperses — its purpose was to protect the debate's integrity, and the debate's integrity just received a legitimate input.

failureEffect.narrative: You do not have the conviction to cast a vote that will hold for fifty years.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The podium does not recognize unauthorized speakers.

stages.title: The Unfinished Debate
stages.description: The sealed vault where the vote's outcome has been suspended for fifty years. The Unfinished Debate is not a spirit. It is a decision that was made but never enacted — three to two, in favor of exposing the water spirit's infiltration of the Verdant Word. It fights because a decision that has been unmade for fifty years does not become gentle when it is finally read. It fights because the people who prevented its enactment are not here to face it, and the party is.

name: The Hall of Ancestral Echoes
description: The coronation cathedral beneath Khaz-Morad, where the Forge-Kings received ancestral blessing. The succession crisis disturbed the ancestors. They rose. They have been conducting a trial ever since. Both factions sent delegations. Neither delegation returned with a favorable verdict.

lore.text: The Hall requires a unanimous verdict from the Stone Jurors before it will recognize a new king. The Jurors have been evaluating claimants for four years. The verdict has been unanimous throughout. It is not the verdict either faction wanted.

The Disowned were stripped from the ancestral record because they complicated the factional narratives — Ironvein ancestors who traded with the surface against the Loyalists' isolationist position, Goldcrown ancestors who refused direct orders from the council that the Usurpers claim to represent. The Hall restored their testimony when the seals broke. They have been making their case at considerable volume ever since.

High Judge Thorgrim has evaluated one hundred and seventeen claimants in four years. His notes on each are detailed, specific, and damning. The criteria for legitimate succession are not about bloodline — they are about demonstrated judgment, stewardship, and the ability to hear counsel that conflicts with self-interest. Neither faction has produced a candidate who meets the second criterion. None have met the third.

The third heir named in the Forge's sealed assessment is not a soldier or a politician. The name is a merchant's child — the grandchild of a trading house that operated between Khaz-Morad and the Silver Vale for two centuries before the succession crisis closed the roads. The trading house's records are in the Guildhall of Silent Trade. The child does not know about the claim. The Guildmaster Vane does, which is part of why the gates are sealed.

stages.title: The Outer Halls
stages.description: The entrance halls where the factional delegations fought their way in and stopped. Echo Conscripts from both sides still hold positions against each other and against any new arrivals. The Ancestor Shades watch from the alcoves with the expression of people who have seen this before and found it tiresome then too.

successEffect.narrative: The Ironvein Ancestral Signet carries a mark that the Hall recognizes — not the modern Loyalist faction's mark, but the original Ironvein line's mark, from before the crisis. Two Echo Conscripts from the Ironvein side lower their weapons. They are not standing down. They are deferring to a credential that outranks their factional orders. The Ancestor Shades in the alcoves straighten slightly.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing the Hall recognizes as authorized.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The outer halls do not acknowledge the unauthorized.

successEffect.narrative: You address the Ancestor Shades directly — not as enemies, as witnesses. Holy magic carries the tone of formal address, the same frequency the coronation ceremonies used. Two Shades turn toward you fully. They are not hostile to someone who knows how to speak to the dead correctly. They are assessing. The Echo Conscripts nearest them pause, uncertain whether this counts as an interruption to the proceedings.

failureEffect.narrative: The Shades do not respond to informal address.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Hall does not open its proceedings to those without the right form of address.

stages.title: The Judgment Hall
stages.description: The main chamber where the coronation ceremonies took place. The Stone Jurors stand in their original positions. The Disowned move through the chamber giving testimony that no one living can hear. The trial has been in continuous session for four years.

successEffect.narrative: The signet activates a sealed alcove off the main chamber — the record room where Thorgrim's assessments are stored. The door opens. The Disowned nearest it go still. Whatever is in the record room is relevant to their testimony and they know it. One of them turns toward you with what might be, in a face that has been dead for varying amounts of time, something like hope.

failureEffect.narrative: The record room remains sealed.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The judgment hall does not reveal its records to the unauthorized.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The record room is open. Thorgrim's complete assessment is inside — including the name of the third heir. The High Judge knows you have read it before you reach the dais. He is already turning to face you.

stages.title: The Central Dais
stages.description: High Judge Thorgrim at the coronation dais with the Ancestor Shades arrayed behind him. The trial is at the sentencing phase. The party is the current defendant. Thorgrim does not look like someone who has been waiting four years. He looks like someone who has been waiting four hundred.

successEffect.narrative: Thorgrim recites the succession criteria before the fight begins — stewardship, judgment, the ability to hear unwelcome counsel. He is not asking whether you meet them. He is giving you the opportunity to demonstrate whether you understand what they mean. A party with sufficient conviction answers correctly. One Ancestor Shade steps back. Thorgrim notes it without comment.

failureEffect.narrative: You do not meet the criteria. Thorgrim proceeds.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The dais does not acknowledge those who cannot answer the opening question.

successEffect.narrative: You present the Ancestral Verdict alongside the Ironvein Signet — the Forge's written assessment and the lineage credential that opens the record room. Thorgrim receives them in silence. The Ancestor Shades behind him confer in whatever language the dead use for deliberation. One of them disperses — not in defeat, but in acknowledgment. The case has been heard. The trial continues, but its terms have changed.

failureEffect.narrative: You present the verdict but cannot demonstrate the conviction to stand behind it. Thorgrim has seen claimants present documents before. He requires more than the paper.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without the written verdict from the Forge, there is no case to present to the Hall.

stages.title: The Record Room
stages.description: Thorgrim's complete assessment — one hundred and seventeen claimants evaluated against criteria that have not changed in four hundred years. The third heir named in the final entry is a merchant's grandchild whose trading house records are in the Guildhall of Silent Trade. Thorgrim follows you into the record room because the assessment is not for general circulation. It is for the named heir specifically. You are not the named heir. He intends to ensure the record goes to the right person.

name: The Stone-Tusk Forward Camp
description: A fortified Orcish mercenary encampment on the Red Plains beyond the Northern Highlands. The Stone-Tusk Clan was hired by the Goldcrown Usurpers five years ago and never paid. They seized this valley as collateral. They are still here.

lore.text: The Stone-Tusk Clan fought at Stone Tooth Pass for three months on Goldcrown terms. They were effective. The Usurpers stopped payment when the front stalled, calculating that Orcs stranded in a foreign valley were less dangerous than Orcs paid and free to go home. The calculation was wrong on both counts.

Krog's raiding doctrine is specific: supply lines only, no settlements, no civilians. He wants payment, not a war. He has maintained this discipline for five years through warriors who are increasingly less interested in the distinction. The discipline is holding. The discipline is the only thing preventing this from becoming something much worse.

The Merchant Guild knew the Usurpers were going to default on the Stone-Tusk contract. The default was discussed in the same correspondence where the Verdant Word dissolution was anticipated. The Guild calculated that unpaid Orcs raiding supply lines would drive up transport costs, which would increase the value of the goods locked in the Guildhall. Krog does not know this. The annotated ledger in his possession gets him most of the way there.

The Goldcrown Usurpers hired the Stone-Tusk Clan specifically because they intended to default. An Orcish mercenary company stranded in the highlands creates a threat that justifies ongoing military spending, which justifies ongoing taxation, which funds the war effort without requiring a battlefield victory. Krog was not a soldier. He was a budget line. He has spent five years becoming something more inconvenient than that.

stages.title: The Outer Perimeter
stages.description: The camp's outer ring is where the Plain-Runners train and patrol. The training dummies are worn down to stumps. They have had five years of practice and nothing to spend it on except raiding and waiting.

successEffect.narrative: The Vulture Company ledger has Goldcrown financial records in it — the same payment structure that appears in the Stone-Tusk contract. A Plain-Runner reads the relevant entries and calls back to the tent line. Two of them stand down and wait. They want to see where this goes.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no paperwork the clan recognizes. The Plain-Runners advance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The perimeter does not open for those without standing.

successEffect.narrative: The Guild Seal gets a reaction the Plain-Runners were not expecting from themselves — recognition, not deference. The Tusk-Brother nearest you takes the seal and looks at it for a long moment. The Guild's name appears in the contract's payment routing. He steps back. He wants Krog to see this.

failureEffect.narrative: The seal means nothing to fighters who have not been paid.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The perimeter does not distinguish between kinds of visitor.

successEffect.narrative: You present the ledger and read the camp while you do it — the training ground wear patterns, the supply tent organization, the specific exhaustion of people who have been disciplined for five years past the point where discipline feels worth it. You pitch the approach correctly. The Plain-Runners nearest the gate step back. A runner goes to the command tent. Krog will want to see this.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the ledger but you read the situation wrong — too aggressive, too uncertain, the wrong tone for a clan that values precision. The Plain-Runners advance anyway.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without the contract records, there is no opening worth attempting.

successEffect.narrative: The eastern palisade has a gap where two posts have rotted at the base — enough space to hook a rope and go over quietly. The Plain-Runners patrol the perimeter in a fixed rotation. You time it. One of them completes his circuit to find you already inside.

failureEffect.narrative: The palisade is sound and the Plain-Runners are circling. The main gate is the only approach.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The palisade is sound and the Plain-Runners are circling. The main gate is the only approach.

stages.title: The Command Tent
stages.description: The inner camp where the Bone-Guards hold position and the Blood-Shamans work. The shamans have been reading the same wound patterns for five years — raiding injuries, training injuries, the specific damage profile of a clan that is disciplined but running low on reasons to stay that way.

successEffect.narrative: The Blood-Shaman nearest you stops when you enter. She is reading you — not your intentions, your wounds, your recent history written in your body. Holy magic responds to this in kind, the same fundamental register. She speaks to the Bone-Guards beside her. They do not lower their weapons but they do not advance. She is telling them something about what she read. The fight starts later than it would have.

failureEffect.narrative: The shaman reads you and finds nothing that gives her pause.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The command tent does not slow for those without the right frequency.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The ledger is enough for the Bone-Guard captain to send for Krog directly rather than fighting first. The Parley Grounds are behind the command tent. Krog is already there.

stages.title: The Chieftain's Throne
stages.description: Krog at the camp's center, beside the Stone-Tusk ancestral totem he carried from the Red Plains. The totem is not decorative. It is the clan's accounting — every contract taken, every contract honored, every debt owed. It has one entry outstanding. It has been outstanding for five years.

successEffect.narrative: You make the case to Krog before the fight starts — not that he should stand down, but that you understand what was done to him and who did it. He listens. He does not agree to anything. But one Bone-Guard steps back, which in Stone-Tusk terms means Krog has signaled that this conversation has standing. The fight starts with one fewer person trying to end it.

failureEffect.narrative: Krog hears you out and starts the fight anyway. He is not unreasonable. He is owed money.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The throne does not entertain visitors without standing.

stages.title: The Parley Grounds
stages.description: The cleared ground behind the command tent where the Stone-Tusk hold formal proceedings. Krog meets you here because the ledger is evidence and evidence changes the terms of engagement in Stone-Tusk culture. He fights anyway — because the debt is still outstanding, because five years of discipline requires an outlet, and because he wants to know if you are worth the trouble of the conversation that comes after.

name: The Gallery of Fractured Light
description: A sealed elven sanctuary deep in the Whispering Woods, accessible from the Whispering Willow Shrine's root-bridges. The Luminary Exiles sealed these bridges fifty years ago when the Verdant Word dissolved. They are not defending the Gallery from enemies. They are quarantining it from the Fraying — the magical residue of every broken oath in the Vale. The party carries that residue with them. The elves know.

lore.text: The Fraying is not metaphor. It is the visible residue of broken oaths — blackened veins in living wood, static in still air, the specific discoloration that appears in nature magic when the pact that empowered it has been severed. The Verdant Word's dissolution produced the largest single Fraying event in the Vale's recorded history. The elves saw it from the canopy. They sealed the bridges four days later.

The Luminary Exiles do not consider themselves cowards. They consider themselves the last people who understood what the Verdant Word's magic actually was. The Order learned their binding techniques here, in this Gallery. When the Order broke those bindings, the Elves felt it in the First Tree's roots. They chose quarantine because they knew what the alternative looked like. They had seen it three hundred years ago when the original marsh binding failed.

High Keeper Elara sealed the bridges herself. She was the same Elara who had signed the halfling pact with Lyra three hundred years earlier — old enough to remember when the Vale's oaths held, old enough to recognize what their breaking meant. When she withdrew into the First Tree, she left a fragment of herself behind at the Shrine of the First Oath. She has not acknowledged this decision in fifty years. The fragment has been maintaining the halfling pact alone since the day she left.

The Architect of Decay is not a person. It is the Fraying given intent — the accumulated weight of every broken oath in the Vale, shaped by the water spirit's will into something that acts. The sigil the Verdant Word found in the Spire's restricted archive was not a signature. It was a fingerprint. The unnamed third entity did not infiltrate the Order. It grew inside the Order's broken promises over two hundred years until it had enough coherence to act. The elves knew this was possible. It is why they sealed the bridges. They could not stop the Architect. They could only ensure it would not have the First Tree.

stages.title: The Root Bridges
stages.description: The bridges between the Whispering Woods and the Gallery proper. The Canopy Pruners work the outer approaches systematically — not searching for enemies, cataloguing contamination levels. The Silence-Weavers move behind them, treating what the Pruners find. The party registers as heavily contaminated.

successEffect.narrative: The Verdant Word Seal carries the Order's original authorization frequency — the same frequency the elves taught them to use three hundred years ago. One Canopy Pruner reads it and pauses. The seal is contaminated at the edges, Frayed like everything else the Order touched, but the core signature is still intact. The Pruner steps back to report. The report will not help you. But it buys a moment.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing the Gallery's intake protocol recognizes as authorized.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The bridges do not distinguish between kinds of contamination.

successEffect.narrative: Nature magic from outside the Gallery has a specific resonance — the same base frequency the elves use, shaped differently by three centuries of human adaptation. The Silence-Weavers register this and recalibrate their approach. They do not stand down. But they treat you as a corruption that can potentially be cleansed rather than one that must be cut out. The distinction is narrow. It is not nothing.

failureEffect.narrative: The bridge approach registers you as standard contamination.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Pruners work without differentiation.

stages.title: The Council Spires
stages.description: The inner Gallery where the Verdant Word's founders were taught. The teaching chambers are still arranged for instruction. The Mirror-Wardens hold the perimeter. The Fraying-Touched move in the lower chambers below the teaching floor, visible through the glass — containment patients watching visitors with the specific attention of people who recognize the contamination the visitors carry because they carry it too.

successEffect.narrative: The Fraying-Touched respond to shadow magic — not as a threat, as recognition. They carry the Fraying's shadow residue in their own bodies. A character with shadow magic reads that residue directly and understands: the Fraying is not random corruption. It has a pattern. It has been moving in a specific direction for two hundred years, accumulating in specific places, building toward something. The Touched look at you with the expression of people who have known this for a long time and have had no one to tell.

failureEffect.narrative: The Fraying-Touched watch you from below the glass without responding.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The spires offer nothing to those who cannot read the shadow frequency.

successEffect.narrative: The Oath Stone pulses when you enter the teaching chambers. The pact frequency — Lyra's original binding, the halfling elders' three centuries of maintained song — resonates with something in the First Tree's root network beneath the floor. The Mirror-Wardens feel it and orient toward the pulse rather than toward you. Something deep in the Gallery is responding to the Oath Stone. Something that has not responded to anything in fifty years.

failureEffect.narrative: The teaching chambers are stone and glass and old wood.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The First Tree does not respond to those without the pact frequency.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The First Tree's response to the Oath Stone opens the root passage to the Heart Tree Chamber. Elara is already there. She has known you were coming since the Oath Stone entered the Gallery. She has been deciding for two days whether to collapse the passage. She did not.

stages.title: The Heart Tree Chamber
stages.description: Elara at the center of the First Tree's root system, maintaining the seals that have kept the Fraying out for fifty years. She does not speak when the party enters. She has already decided. The fight begins because she cannot afford the alternative — not because she wants it.

successEffect.narrative: You name the Fragment — not as an accusation, as an acknowledgment. You tell Elara what the Fragment has been doing at the Shrine for fifty years. Not the pact maintenance, which she knows. The loneliness, which she has not allowed herself to calculate. One Mirror-Warden disperses. Elara has not moved. But the seal nearest the root passage has grown three new rings since you started speaking, which means the tree is listening.

failureEffect.narrative: Elara does not engage with arguments she has already heard from herself.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Heart Tree Chamber does not pause.

stages.title: The Fragment's Reckoning
stages.description: The Oath Stone does not summon the Fragment. It resonates with her — the pact frequency reaching through fifty years of separation to the part of Elara that stayed. The Fragment manifests as a secondary presence in the chamber, not physical, not hostile, simply present in the way that unresolved decisions are present. Elara fights with full power. Her healing is halved because the Fragment disrupts her concentration every time she tries to justify the withdrawal to herself. She has been making that justification for fifty years. It has never been more difficult than it is right now.

name: The Ironvein Relay
description: A dwarven supply convoy moving through the foothills of Stone Tooth Pass, protected by Ironvein Loyalist veterans who have kept the route open through four years of succession war. The cargo is sealed. The veterans are not corrupt — they are disciplined, oath-bound, and prepared to kill anyone who threatens the shipment.

lore.text: The Ironvein Relay has moved continuously for four years. The veterans never stop in the same place twice. The cargo manifests are sealed and countersigned by Supply Captain Vedra personally. No one below her rank has ever seen what is actually being shipped.

The Frostbite Conscripts were not always undead. They were living soldiers who took an oath of preservation — to keep the route open until the war ended. The war did not end. The oath did not expire. The soldiers did not survive long enough to be released from it.

The Goldcrown faction has intercepted two Ironvein shipments in the past year. Neither interception found weapons or supplies. The crates contained records — documentation of agreements between the Ironvein Loyalists and a third party whose name does not appear in any official ledger. The Goldcrown commanders who read those records were reassigned to the front lines within a week.

Vedra's field orders are not military orders. They are reporting instructions — schedules for when the convoy will pass specific waypoints, formatted for someone who needs to know where the shipment is without being seen near it. The Ironvein succession war is not a distraction from the Architect's work. It is the work. The cargo has never mattered. The route has.

stages.title: The Frozen Approach
stages.description: The convoy's perimeter is held by Frostbite Conscripts — oath-bound dwarven dead who patrol the ice-covered foothills with the same discipline they had in life. Ironvein Outriders sweep the ridgeline on mounted patrol, cutting off flanking approaches.

successEffect.narrative: The Loyalist Seal carries the correct counter-signature. Two Conscripts halt their patrol and hold position. They are not standing down — they are deferring, briefly, to an authority they still recognize. The Outriders have not seen the seal. They have not slowed.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credential the perimeter recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The foothills offer no cover. The Conscripts have already marked you.

successEffect.narrative: The Outriders run a predictable circuit. One passes the far ridge on a schedule and won't return for several minutes. You move before the window closes.

failureEffect.narrative: The patrol pattern is irregular enough that you can't find a clean gap. They've trained against ambush.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You see soldiers moving but can't read the pattern in time.

stages.title: The Convoy Interior
stages.description: The convoy's core is protected by Tide Wardens who use channelled cold magic to slow and disorient intruders, and Siege Callers who coordinate suppressing fire from elevated positions on the supply wagons. The Wardens and Callers work in concert — Chilled targets are easier to pin, and Electrified targets burn through resources before the fight is half done.

successEffect.narrative: A fire-aspected skill disrupts the Wardens' channelling before combat begins. The cold in the air thins noticeably. The Wardens will need time to re-establish their link — your first round is clean.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no fire-aspected skill to disrupt the channelling.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Wardens are already linked. The cold hits the moment you breach the perimeter.

successEffect.narrative: The Vulture Company ledger contains the Relay's secondary contract — the Siege Callers are mercenaries, not veterans. Confronting one with proof of the ledger's contents is enough. He steps down from the wagon and leaves without a word. The remaining Caller watches him go and does not move to stop him.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the ledger but not the reach to leverage it under pressure.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Siege Callers hold their positions. They were paid in advance.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The Loyalist Seal opens more than the outer checkpoint. A sealed compartment in the lead wagon, marked with Vedra's counter-signature, requires exactly this credential to access. Someone wanted this to be findable by the right person.

stages.title: The Observation Deck
stages.description: Supply Captain Vedra commands from the lead wagon's upper platform, flanked by the convoy's last Tide Wardens. She fights with the deliberate economy of a soldier who has kept this route open for four years through attrition — draining what she cannot break, buffing her remaining soldiers before engaging directly.

successEffect.narrative: You present the Loyalist Seal and name the counter-signature. Vedra goes still. She dismisses the Wardens with a word — not because you've convinced her, but because she understands you know enough to make this personal. Whatever happens next, she wants no witnesses.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the seal but Vedra reads the challenge in your face before you can frame it. She signals the Wardens to hold their positions.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Vedra does not negotiate with people who announce themselves by breaking through her perimeter.

stages.title: The Sealed Compartment
stages.description: The compartment is not a weapons cache. It is a document archive — sealed orders, countersigned route maps, and correspondence addressed to no named recipient. Reading it takes time. Vedra finds you before you finish.

successEffect.narrative: The orders are not combat instructions. They are waypoint schedules — precise timings for when the convoy will pass specific locations, formatted as reporting intervals. The recipient is identified only by a sigil you have seen before, pressed into the corner of every document. Someone has been tracking this convoy's position. Vedra arrives before you can read further.

failureEffect.narrative: The orders are not combat instructions. They are waypoint schedules — precise timings for when the convoy will pass specific locations, formatted as reporting intervals. The recipient is identified only by a sigil you have seen before, pressed into the corner of every document. Someone has been tracking this convoy's position. Vedra arrives before you can read further.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The orders are not combat instructions. They are waypoint schedules — precise timings for when the convoy will pass specific locations, formatted as reporting intervals. The recipient is identified only by a sigil you have seen before, pressed into the corner of every document. Someone has been tracking this convoy's position. Vedra arrives before you can read further.

name: The Vulture's Roost
description: The Vulture Company's primary command post, carved into the cliffs above the Silver Vale trade routes. Captain Vane runs his raiding operation from here — the contracts, the ledgers, the chain of command that has kept the Vale's roads impassable for four years. The Company isn't hiding anymore. They're managing.

lore.text: The Roost was a minor waystation before the Royal Guard withdrew. Vane didn't build it — he inherited it when the garrison left and the previous occupants didn't survive the transition. The garrison's records were still here when he arrived. He has not shared what they contained.

The Vulture Company's contracts are not random. The shipments they intercept are specifically selected — grain convoys pass unmolested while Verdant Word supply lines are hit every time. Someone is telling them which wagons to take and which to leave. The Company calls this good intelligence. The merchants who've noticed the pattern call it something else.

Vane keeps two sets of ledgers. The one his Strike Captains have seen shows standard raiding income. The second, locked in his personal quarters, shows payments from a source he refers to only as the Contractor. The amounts dwarf the raiding revenue. The Vulture Company is not a mercenary band that raids for profit. It is a funded operation with a specific mandate, and raiding is the cover.

The blood-oath ceremony is not a Vulture Company tradition. It is a Stonetusk one — a contract ritual that makes debt binding across both parties in perpetuity. Vane performed it with Chieftain Krog's envoy three years ago in exchange for safe passage through Orc territory. He has not honored the debt. The Stonetusk Clan knows. They have been waiting to see if he will. The Contractor's payments are the reason he hasn't — the Contractor needs the Orcish alliance broken, not mended.

stages.title: The Outer Perimeter
stages.description: Wing Scouts hold the cliffside approaches in rotating pairs, and Strike Captains anchor the main gate. The Company runs a tight perimeter — the Scouts bleed targets before the Captains close in to finish.

successEffect.narrative: The ledger carries Vane's counter-signature on the inside cover. The Wing Scouts recognize it and wave you through without a word — the Company doesn't question people who know the captain's mark. The Strike Captains at the gate have not seen you yet.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credential the perimeter recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The cliffside offers no approach the Scouts haven't already mapped.

successEffect.narrative: The wind off the cliff face is constant and loud enough to cover movement. One Strike Captain has his back to the approach. You're inside his perimeter before he knows you're there.

failureEffect.narrative: The wind is in your favour but the Captains are watching the right angles. They've done this longer than you.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You can't find an opening before the Scouts mark your position.

stages.title: The Command Compound
stages.description: The Roost's interior is anchored by Ironbound veterans at the chokepoints and a Quartermaster who keeps their stamina topped and their damage up. The Ironbound are the wall — the Quartermaster is why the wall holds.

successEffect.narrative: The supply cache is separate from the command tent, and it's where the Quartermaster spends most of his time. You reach it before he does. Without his stores he has nothing to offer the Ironbound — he retreats rather than fight empty-handed.

failureEffect.narrative: The Quartermaster is already at his post. You can't reach the cache without going through him.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You don't find the cache in time.

successEffect.narrative: The Guild Seal is proof of something the Ironbound have suspected: the Contractor is the Guild, and the Guild's contracts don't cover dying in a compound fight. One Ironbound steps aside. He doesn't look ashamed. He looks like he's been waiting for a reason.

failureEffect.narrative: The seal means something but not enough — the Ironbound aren't paid to reason through implications under pressure.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You have nothing the Ironbound recognize as authority.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The ledger gets you past the outer gate, but it also gets you into Vane's personal quarters — a room the Strike Captains know exists but have never entered. The lock uses the same counter-signature as the ledger's cover. He built himself a way out and left the key in his most dangerous document.

stages.title: The Chief's Throne
stages.description: Vane commands from the cliff's highest platform — a chair, a table covered in contracts, and a view of every road he's made impassable. He fights like the officer he used to be: pressure first, then attrition, then the killing blow when you're already spent.

successEffect.narrative: You lay the Guild Seal on his table and name the Contractor. Vane is quiet for a moment. Then he dismisses the Strike Captains — not because you've beaten him, but because what comes next is between him and whoever sent you. He doesn't want witnesses to what he's about to say before the fight starts.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the seal but Vane doesn't let you finish the sentence. He's heard enough accusations to know how to shut them down.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Vane doesn't negotiate with people who break into his compound.

stages.title: The Blood-Oath Chamber
stages.description: Vane's private quarters contain two things: a second set of ledgers and a Stonetusk contract marker on the wall — a blood-oath token, half-completed, the Orcish side of the seal pressed but the human side left blank. He signed the ritual and never finished it. Vane arrives before you can read everything.

successEffect.narrative: The contract is written in both Trade Common and Stonetusk script. Vane agreed to honor an Orcish debt — safe passage, a share of the raiding income, acknowledgment of the clan's territorial claim — in exchange for access to the northern passes. He signed it. The Stonetusk envoy pressed his mark. The debt is real and it is unpaid. The Contractor's ledger entries start two weeks after the oath was made. Vane arrives before you finish reading.

failureEffect.narrative: The contract is written in both Trade Common and Stonetusk script. Vane agreed to honor an Orcish debt — safe passage, a share of the raiding income, acknowledgment of the clan's territorial claim — in exchange for access to the northern passes. He signed it. The Stonetusk envoy pressed his mark. The debt is real and it is unpaid. The Contractor's ledger entries start two weeks after the oath was made. Vane arrives before you finish reading.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The contract is written in both Trade Common and Stonetusk script. Vane agreed to honor an Orcish debt — safe passage, a share of the raiding income, acknowledgment of the clan's territorial claim — in exchange for access to the northern passes. He signed it. The Stonetusk envoy pressed his mark. The debt is real and it is unpaid. The Contractor's ledger entries start two weeks after the oath was made. Vane arrives before you finish reading.

name: The Blood-Oath Tribunal
description: A neutral field between the Dwarven Holds and the Silver Vale where the Stonetusk Clan has called the factions of the succession crisis to settle their debts. Chieftain Krog is not here to negotiate — he is here to collect. The arbitration has been armed since the first delegation arrived.

lore.text: The Tribunal field has hosted three prior arbitrations. The first two ended in settlement. The third ended when one delegation's escorts decided the outcome before the judges finished deliberating. The bones from that session were never cleared. Krog chose this site deliberately. He wants everyone to remember what happens when the process fails.

The Stonetusk Clan is owed three things: safe passage through the northern passes, a share of the Ironvein trade revenue, and acknowledgment of their territorial claim on the lowland hunting grounds. The Goldcrown faction has contested all three for five years. The Ironvein faction agreed to the second two and has been using Goldcrown's objection as cover to avoid paying the first. Krog knows this. He has been watching both factions lie to his face for five years. He is done watching.

The Unpaid Ledger the Stonetusk Clan carries is not a record of what they are owed. It is a record of what they have already collected — raids on Goldcrown supply lines, tolls on unauthorized pass traffic, the slow attrition of a creditor who has given up asking politely. By the Tribunal's accounting, the debt is nearly settled. What Krog actually wants is the acknowledgment. The Orcs do not need the gold. They need the dwarves to say out loud that the contract was real.

The Third Heir's claim was in the Stonetusk records before it appeared in any dwarven document. Krog's envoy identified the legitimate bloodline four years ago during a routine contract review — the Heir's grandmother had signed a territorial agreement with the Clan in exchange for safe passage during the succession crisis of her generation. The Stonetusk Clan has known who the rightful heir is longer than anyone. They have been waiting to use it. Presenting the signet at the Tribunal is not a surprise to Krog. It is the move he has been waiting for someone to make.

stages.title: The Outer Arbitration Ground
stages.description: The Tribunal's perimeter is held by Stonetusk Plain-Runners who enforce the boundary between delegations, and Tusk-Brothers who guarantee no one leaves before the debt is acknowledged. The party registers as an unknown party — neither delegation nor witness.

successEffect.narrative: The Unpaid Ledger carries the Stonetusk seal on the cover. The Plain-Runners recognize it immediately and step back — anyone carrying this document has either stolen it from a corpse or is here on legitimate business, and the Clan's protocol is to let the Chieftain decide which. Two runners peel off to report. The Tusk-Brothers have not moved.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing the perimeter recognizes as authorization.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Plain-Runners have already marked your position. Unknown parties do not pass the boundary unchallenged.

successEffect.narrative: The Tusk-Brothers respect one thing above credentials: someone who doesn't flinch when challenged. You hold your position without breaking eye contact until one of them steps aside. It is not a concession. It is an acknowledgment that this fight will cost something.

failureEffect.narrative: The Tusk-Brothers read the hesitation. They close the distance.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The boundary is absolute. Everyone who doesn't belong here gets removed.

stages.title: The Inner Council Ring
stages.description: The arbitration's core is guarded by Bone-Guards anchoring the council perimeter and a Blood-Shaman who maintains the oath-binding ritual that keeps the session legitimate. The Shaman is the reason the dwarven delegations haven't fled — while the ritual holds, no faction can leave without forfeiting their claim. Disrupt it and the council dissolves into a free fight.

successEffect.narrative: The Oath Stone from the Shrine of the First Oath resonates with the Shaman's ritual — it is older than the Stonetusk binding and the Shaman recognizes it. He steps back from the circle without a word. The oath-binding holds on its own for now, but the Bone-Guards are watching you with new attention.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing the ritual recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Shaman does not acknowledge outsiders during the binding. The Bone-Guards enforce this.

successEffect.narrative: You speak directly to the council before the Bone-Guards can move. One guard pauses — not because the argument was persuasive, but because interrupting a formal address during an active binding is a protocol violation and he knows it. He holds position while the rest close in.

failureEffect.narrative: The council does not recognize your standing to address it. The Bone-Guards do not wait for a second attempt.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You have no standing here. The guards enforce that immediately.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The Third Heir's Signet stops everything. The Shaman recognizes the seal before you've finished drawing it — the Stonetusk records have carried this bloodline for four years. The Bone-Guards step back. Krog has been summoned. This is the move he was waiting for.

stages.title: The Chieftain's Judgment
stages.description: Krog does not fight from a throne. He fights from the center of the field, in front of both delegations, because he wants everyone to see what happens to people who waste his time. The Bone-Guards flank him. This is not an ambush — it is an execution of process.

successEffect.narrative: The Blood-Oath Token from the Roost is proof that the Vulture Company's debt to the Stonetusk Clan has been acknowledged. Krog takes it, reads the seal, and sets it on the arbitration table. He dismisses the Bone-Guards with a gesture — the formal part of the proceeding is over. What remains is between him and you, and he intends to conduct it honestly.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the token but Krog's patience for presentations ran out before you arrived. He has been waiting five years. He is not waiting any longer.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Krog does not negotiate with people who haven't done their homework.

stages.title: The Legitimate Claim
stages.description: The Stonetusk records are produced. The Heir's bloodline is verified against the grandmother's territorial agreement. Krog reads the signet against his records and finds them matching. He then looks at you, and the session becomes something else entirely — a test of whether you understand what you're holding.

successEffect.narrative: Krog verifies the signet against the Stonetusk territorial record — the match is exact. He sets both documents on the table and looks at you for a long moment. Then he says: the Clan will honor the coronation if the party can prove they understand what they're asking for. The Tusk-Brothers move to flank. This is the proof.

failureEffect.narrative: Krog verifies the signet against the Stonetusk territorial record — the match is exact. He sets both documents on the table and looks at you for a long moment. Then he says: the Clan will honor the coronation if the party can prove they understand what they're asking for. The Tusk-Brothers move to flank. This is the proof.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Krog verifies the signet against the Stonetusk territorial record — the match is exact. He sets both documents on the table and looks at you for a long moment. Then he says: the Clan will honor the coronation if the party can prove they understand what they're asking for. The Tusk-Brothers move to flank. This is the proof.

name: The Frayed Outpost
description: A sealed sub-basement beneath the Royal Palace where the High Council of the Verdant Word held its final session fifty years ago. The vote was never completed. The councillors who died in the coup are still here, still deliberating, still preventing anyone from reaching the record of what was actually decided.

lore.text: The sub-basement was sealed from the outside the same night the coup occurred. The Royal Guard posted at the entrance were reassigned within the week. No official record acknowledges the chamber exists. The iron-bound transaction ledgers lining the walls contain every contract the Verdant Word signed in the last three hundred years. Someone wanted those records inaccessible more than they wanted the councillors dead.

The Echo Councillors are not haunting the chamber. They are completing the session. The vote on the Entropy threat was four votes in favour, three opposed, and one abstention when the coup began. The abstention belonged to a councillor who left the room to retrieve a document. She never returned. The remaining seven have been waiting for a quorum that will never arrive. They cannot close the session without it. They cannot leave without closing the session.

High Speaker Valerius is not the same entity encountered in the Spire. The Spire holds the version of Valerius that survived and was corrupted. This chamber holds the version that died in the coup — the one who called the vote, who believed the threat was real, who never knew the outcome. He has been presiding over an unfinished deliberation for fifty years. He does not know the Architect won. He is still trying to stop it.

stages.title: The Outer Stacks
stages.description: The chamber's outer hall is still staffed — Silent Scribes move between the iron-bound shelves marking anything that approaches, and Echo Judges maintain the session's procedural integrity with force. The quills have not stopped scratching.

successEffect.narrative: The Verdant Word Seal carries the correct session authorization. Two Scribes pause mid-transcription and defer — the seal predates the coup and the Judges recognize it as valid entry credentials. They do not stand down, but they stop marking you. The Judges have taken positions at the inner door.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credential the session recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Unauthorized access to an active council session is an executable offence. The Scribes begin marking immediately.

successEffect.narrative: You invoke procedural right of address — the formal request that any witness may present evidence to an active session. One Echo Judge hesitates. The protocol is fifty years old but the session never closed, which means the protocol still applies. He steps back to allow presentation. The others do not.

failureEffect.narrative: The floor is not yours to request. The Judges enforce this without ceremony.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The session is not accepting new testimony. The Scribes have already logged your presence as a disruption.

stages.title: The Deliberation Chamber
stages.description: The inner chamber is where the vote stalled. Whisper Mages enforce the silence protocols that kept deliberations secret in life, and Vault Wardens seal the exits the same way they did the night of the coup. The ambient Fraying makes every mana expenditure here cost more than it should.

successEffect.narrative: The Athenaeum Scroll is an external record of the session — a backup copy filed by a Scrivener who didn't survive the coup. One Whisper Mage recognizes the filing seal and goes still. An external record means the session's secrecy was already broken decades ago. She has been enforcing a confidentiality that no longer exists. She does not fight. She simply stops.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing the confidentiality protocol recognizes as authorization.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The silence protocols are absolute. Mana expenditure in this chamber is logged as a procedural violation.

successEffect.narrative: The Vault Wardens sealed these exits fifty years ago on direct order. You make clear that order is no longer operative. One Warden holds your gaze for a long moment — then steps aside. He does not concede the point. He concedes that the argument requires more force to settle than he has been allocated.

failureEffect.narrative: The exits are sealed. The order has not been rescinded. The Wardens will not discuss it further.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The doors do not open. The Wardens do not explain why.

stages.title: The Speaker's Podium
stages.description: High Speaker Valerius presides from the head of the council table, still waiting for quorum, still calling the vote that cannot close. He treats the party as a disruption to be silenced and a mana drain as the appropriate response to unauthorized presence during active deliberation.

successEffect.narrative: You present the Verdant Word Seal and declare the absent councillor's vote — in favour, as her later writings in the Athenaeum confirm. Valerius goes silent. For a moment the scratching of quills stops entirely. Five votes in favour is a majority. The session can close. He dismisses the Judges. Then he looks at you, and his expression is not relief. He has just realized what a closed vote means: the decision was made. The Architect's pact was rejected. And it happened anyway.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the seal but not the standing to deliver testimony on another councillor's behalf. Valerius calls the chamber to order.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Valerius does not recognize you as a party to these proceedings. The session resumes.

name: The Merchant's Ledger Vault
description: A sealed subterranean archive beneath the Guildhall of Silent Trade, where Guildmaster Vane has kept the records of the Third Heir buried in the merchant lineage files for five years. The Coin-Keepers don't know what they're guarding. Vane has made sure of that.

lore.text: The vault was sealed the same week the Verdant Word dissolved. Vane's official account is that the closure was a precaution against political instability. The actual content of the sealed section — three crates of merchant lineage files cross-referenced against dwarven territorial agreements — suggests the precaution was specifically targeted.

The Third Heir's claim runs through the merchant side of the family — a grandmother who traded across the dwarven passes and signed a territorial compact with the Stonetusk Clan three generations ago. That compact is in this vault. Vane found it five years ago and understood immediately what it meant: whoever holds that document controls the succession. He has been holding it ever since.

The Coin-Keepers are paid by contract, not loyalty. Their clearance levels are structured so that no single guard knows what the others have seen. Vane designed the security around the assumption that someone would eventually try to bribe their way in. He did not design it around the assumption that someone would arrive already knowing what was inside.

stages.title: The Outer Stacks
stages.description: Ledger Clerks patrol the outer stacks in overlapping patterns, and Audit Mages hold the inner entrance behind arcane locks. The Clerks bleed anything that moves without authorization. The Mages silence anything that tries to bypass the locks with magic.

successEffect.narrative: The Guild Seal carries the standard access authorization. Two Clerks step aside — their contract specifies deference to sealed credentials, and the seal is genuine. They don't know it was obtained elsewhere. The Audit Mages have not seen it yet and are not bound by the same clause.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credential the outer stacks recognize.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Unauthorized entry into an active Guild archive is a contract violation. The Clerks treat it as one.

successEffect.narrative: The Audit Mages monitor the primary approach but the stacks themselves are deep enough to move through unseen. You reach the inner entrance before one of them completes their circuit. They'll find the gap on the next pass.

failureEffect.narrative: The Mages cover the approaches thoroughly. You don't find a clean line.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The stacks are too well-lit for undetected movement.

stages.title: The Record Room
stages.description: The inner archive is held by Coin-Keepers anchoring the chokepoints and Audit Mages who silence anything that tries to work around them. The Keepers are immovable. The Mages make sure the Keepers stay that way by stripping the party's options down to whatever they brought that doesn't cost mana.

successEffect.narrative: The Vulture Company Ledger contains the Guild's secondary contract — the one that hired the Company to intercept Verdant Word supply lines. One Coin-Keeper reads the page you open to and sets down his weapon. His contract with the Guild is employment, not complicity. There's a difference, and the ledger makes clear which side of it he's been standing on.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the ledger but the Keepers aren't paid to reason through implications mid-shift.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Coin-Keepers hold their positions. They were retained specifically to avoid being reasoned with.

successEffect.narrative: One Audit Mage breaks position to reinforce the Keepers before you've fully engaged. You step into her path and don't move. She recalculates. A Mage with no line of sight to her target is a Mage wasting a contract hour. She repositions rather than commit.

failureEffect.narrative: The Mages hold their coverage angles. You can't force a gap.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Record Room has no angles that aren't covered.

stages.title: The Vault Door
stages.description: Guildmaster Vane waits at the sealed section with the last of his Coin-Keepers. He does not fight because he enjoys it. He fights because someone reaching this room means his leverage is gone, and a man with nothing to lose is considerably more dangerous than one protecting an asset.

successEffect.narrative: You present the heir_signet and name the lineage. Vane looks at the signet for a long time. Then he dismisses the Coin-Keepers — all of them, including the ones in the outer stacks. His leverage was the secret. The secret is standing in front of him with a signet ring and a complete argument. There is nothing left to guard. He tells them their contracts are fulfilled. Then he turns back to you, and his expression is the specific look of a man who has just realized his five-year plan ended thirty seconds ago.

failureEffect.narrative: You have the signet but Vane doesn't let you finish. He's spent five years anticipating this conversation and he knows which words to cut off.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Vane does not discuss the vault's contents with unauthorized parties. The Coin-Keepers enforce this policy.

name: The Tidebound Forward Basin
description: A flooded crater in the Northern Highlands where the Tidebound Vanguard has established its staging ground for the push south toward the Spire. High Chanter Malacor directs the operation from the Ley Line Altar at the basin's center. The water does not move. The air smells of ozone and rot.
