fallbackEffect.narrative: You have insufficient conviction to anchor a defensive line. The Breakers' acceleration sequence runs uncontested.

stages.title: The Deep Throne Chamber
stages.description: The central chamber where the head smith's spirit was bound to the main anvil four hundred years ago by a Forge-King who wanted a quality standard that would last. It has lasted. The Awakened Foreman does not distinguish between Dwarven tomb-raiders, Ironvein soldiers, and the party — all are unauthorized entry into an active mortuary facility, and his standing order has not been updated since the dissolution. He has been waiting fifty years for someone with the authority to update it.

preCombatOpportunities.name: Present the Verdant Word Seal at the Dais

successEffect.narrative: The Verdant Word seal at the main dais triggers the Foreman's administrative review protocol — the same process the Head Priest used to issue standing orders. The Foreman cannot update his own standing order, but he can acknowledge a credential that qualifies as a supervisory authority. The Scraper Drones, responding to the protocol, reset to standby. The Foreman does not stand down. But he waits for you to make your case before he makes his.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no credential that reaches the administrative review protocol.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Foreman registers you as an unauthorized entry. He opens with earthquake.

successEffect.narrative: The Foreman's opening move is earthquake — a room-wide stun that resets the fight's positioning before his real sequence begins. A party with sufficient conviction and footing can brace against the concussive wave. You do not avoid the earthquake. You absorb it standing. The Foreman's recalibration — designed for a disrupted party — finds you in the same positions he expected you to have vacated. His second action is a direct attack with no setup. You weathered the setup.

failureEffect.narrative: The earthquake connects fully. Your positioning is disrupted before the real fight begins.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot brace against a room-wide stun. The floor wins.

name: The Quarantine Ward
description: A sealed district inside the Human Capital's inner wall, quarantined when citizens began showing signs of Fraying corruption. The quarantine is five years old. The seals have held. The people inside have not died but changed — their shadows move independently, their voices carry in frequencies that damage conviction on extended exposure. Overseer Grell holds the perimeter. His standing order has not changed: contain the damage.

lore.text: The quarantine seals were issued by Captain Hrolf under direct order from the Guildhall — not from the Royal Guard command, not from the Crown, from the Guildhall. The official record shows a Royal Guard authorization. The authorization was backdated. Overseer Grell knows this. He has been following an illegal order for five years because the alternative was no order at all, and no order would have meant the Fraying spreading to the Noble District within a month.

The Shadow-Touched civilians inside the ward are not hostile by nature. Their shadow-independence is a Fraying symptom, not a corruption — the magic in them has come loose, not inverted. Three of them, in the earliest stages of the separation, have been maintaining a kind of peace inside the ward by mediating between the fully changed and the not-yet-changed. Their names are not in any official record. Grell knows their names. He has a list.

The ward's seals are not just keeping the Fraying in. They are concentrating it — the corruption has nowhere to go, so it intensifies. Grell understood this by the end of the first year. His quarterly reports to the Guildhall, marked CLASSIFIED, document the intensification rate and estimate that the ward will reach critical Fraying density within two years of filing. The most recent report is dated three years ago. The Guildhall stopped reading them.

stages.title: The Outer Tents
stages.description: The ward's edge is a camp of improvised barricades, triage tents that have been used as sleeping quarters for two years, and patrol routes that have become grooves in the mud. Shadow-Touched civilians guard the perimeter alongside actual Wardens — it is not always clear which is which from a distance. The Fraying-inflected fear response they carry is not deliberate. It is a symptom. It is no less dangerous for that.

successEffect.narrative: The Royal Guard insignia reads as authorized personnel to the Gatekeepers — they were originally Guard-adjacent before the ward closed, and the credential recognition is still functional. Two of the Shadow-Touched civilians at the perimeter respond to the Gatekeepers' stand-down signal and pull back. They are not choosing to retreat. They are following the last organized authority they knew.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no credential the ward's chain of command recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The outer perimeter has no category for unauthorized ingress except 'threat.'

successEffect.narrative: The Shadow-Touched civilians' aggression is fear-driven — the Fraying corruption has severed their shadow from their judgment, and what is left defaults to threat response. A party projecting sufficient harmonic stability reads as not-a-threat long enough to create a gap. The civilians do not stand down. But they hesitate, which is enough. You move through the hesitation before the Gatekeepers can redirect them.

failureEffect.narrative: The civilians' fear response is too entrenched. Your approach registers as threat regardless of intent.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot project the harmonic stability the ward's occupants need to pause. The threat response fires.

stages.title: The Command Zone
stages.description: The inner ward is where the organized resistance lives — the Rogue Guards who chose to stay when Hrolf rotated out, and the Fraying Manifestations that have grown from five years of concentrated abandonment inside the seals. The Manifestations are not the Architect's agents here. They are the ward's own despair, given physical form. The Rogue Guards are trying to suppress them and failing at a rate that has been documented in Grell's classified reports.

successEffect.narrative: The Guild Seal is the issuing authority for the quarantine order — the Rogue Guards know this, and seeing it in the hands of an unaffiliated party creates a legal and tactical problem they have to stop and assess. One Guard steps back to determine whether you have come to issue a new order or to document the current one. He does not attack while he is assessing. His colleagues watch to see what he determines.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no Guildhall credential. The command zone does not admit non-authorized parties for briefings.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Rogue Guards have been in this command zone for five years with the same standing order. They do not require documentation to defend it.

successEffect.narrative: The Fraying Manifestations' opening combination is shadow_bolt into drain_reserves — they apply shadowed to reduce harmony, then immediately use drain_reserves while harmony is suppressed to maximize the exhaustion debuff's duration. A party with a high enough harmony baseline absorbs the shadowed application without the reduction registering fully. The drain_reserves fires at partial effect. The Manifestations recalculate.

failureEffect.narrative: The shadowed application connects fully. The drain_reserves fires at full effect.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot resist the Manifestation's opening sequence. The double-debuff window opens as intended.

stages.title: The Gatehouse
stages.description: Grell holds the gatehouse entrance — the only point where the inner seal meets the outer wall. He does not look like someone who has been in a quarantine zone for five years. He looks like someone who made a decision five years ago and has not reconsidered it once. His standing order was to contain the damage. He is still containing it. The fact that the damage has been intensifying for four of those five years is documented in his reports. No one read them.

successEffect.narrative: Grell recognizes Hrolf's orders immediately. He reads them in full, standing still for approximately forty-five seconds. Then he looks up and says 'He updated them.' The Fraying Manifestations behind him have been waiting for him to show some sign of breaking. The shift in his bearing — not breaking, but updating — reads to them as loss of coherence. They dissipate. What Grell provides in the form of resolve was the only thing anchoring them to this location.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no document that updates Grell's standing order.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Grell's standing order has not been superseded. He does not break from an order that has not been updated.

successEffect.narrative: Grell knows the quarantine order was issued illegally. He has known for five years. A party with sufficient conviction can make the argument directly — naming the Guild Seal as the issuing authority, naming the backdated authorization, naming every quarterly report that was filed and ignored. Grell does not lower his weapon. But his first attack is delayed by the three seconds it takes him to decide whether this argument changes anything. He decides it does not. But those three seconds are yours.

failureEffect.narrative: You cannot make the argument with sufficient force. Grell has heard it before, from himself.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Grell is not susceptible to arguments he has already made to himself and discarded.

name: The Tidebound Southern Reach
description: A flooded coastal valley south of the Sunken Sanctum, designated in Thrain's report as the Tidebound's primary southern expansion vector. High Chanter Malacor's vanguard has established a forward base here to push the flood toward the Spire of Silent Conviction. The water is unnaturally still. It reflects no light. Something in the valley's ley line is being actively redirected.

lore.text: The valley was designated as the Tidebound's primary target in Thrain's report not because of its strategic value but because of its magical one. The original Verdant Word binding ritual used this valley as one of three anchor points — the place where the promise was spoken, rather than where it was sealed. The Tidebound are not flooding a valley. They are targeting a specific word of an oath they want to unmake.

High Chanter Malacor has been in this valley for five years maintaining the connection to the Sanctum. The connection requires his continuous presence — not his attention, just his presence. He has had five years of uninterrupted time to study the original binding ritual's anchor point at the valley's ley line. He now understands the ritual better than most of the surviving Verdant Word priests. He is using that understanding to unravel it.

The Tidebound's Drowned Scouts were not corrupted by the advance into the valley. They were corrupted by the valley itself — the ley line's redirected magic is leaking into anyone who spends extended time in the water here. Malacor knows this. He has not evacuated the Scouts because their changed state makes them significantly more effective in flooded terrain. His quarterly casualty reports to the Sanctum do not use the word 'corrupted.' They use the phrase 'adapted to operational conditions.'

stages.title: The Outer Reeds
stages.description: The valley's edge is a tangle of submerged stone circles from the original binding site and improvised Tidebound fieldworks built over them. Drowned Scouts wade through the shallows in search patterns that have not changed in five years. Tide Lashers hold the crossing points with the practiced efficiency of soldiers who have had five years to learn exactly which positions are defensible and which are not.

successEffect.narrative: Thrain's complete report includes five years of patrol interval documentation from the Northern Watch's surveillance of this valley. The Scouts have run the same search pattern without modification — the same timing, the same gaps, the same deadspot at the third submerged stone circle where two patrol routes briefly overlap. You enter through the deadspot. Two Scouts are out of position before either can respond. Their search pattern does not include a contingency for the gap being exploited.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no patrol data. The Scouts' search pattern has no visible gap without prior intelligence.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The outer reeds have no unmonitored approach without Thrain's surveillance data.

successEffect.narrative: The Tidebound supply doctrine prioritizes water resistance over fire resistance — their adapted gear is excellent against cold and wet, and poor against combustion. The reed screen the Scouts use for concealment at the crossing ignites cleanly. Two Scouts caught in the cover when it goes are displaced before they can engage. The Tide Lashers pull back to a secondary position. They had not prepared for fire as an approach vector.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no fire skill. The reed screen holds.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The outer reeds are wet. They will not burn without fire magic.

stages.title: The Camp Perimeter
stages.description: The forward base is built on the only dry ground in the valley — a raised shelf of stone above the flood line that the Tidebound have fortified over five years into something that would hold against a military assault. Mist Weavers stand on submerged ruins below the shelf and coordinate the approach suppression. Barnacle Brutes hold the shelf's access points, their fused armor making direct engagement inadvisable.

successEffect.narrative: The Verdant Word seal resonates against the camp's ley line anchor in a frequency the Mist Weavers were trained to recognize as a disruption risk. One Weaver breaks position to assess whether the seal is generating active interference. The assessment takes him out of the suppression line. He returns when he concludes the seal is inert — but the suppression window has closed and the Barnacle Brutes have already committed to a static defensive posture.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no resonant instrument. The camp perimeter's suppression holds.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Mist Weavers' suppression sequence begins on contact. You have nothing to interrupt it.

successEffect.narrative: The shelf's access is designed to funnel attackers into the Barnacle Brutes' shove range — the approach is narrow enough that a standard advance hands positioning to the defenders. A party with sufficient conviction can force the approach at a pace that prevents the Brutes from establishing the shove sequence. You reach the shelf edge before they can reset. The Mist Weavers below are firing upward at an angle. Their first suppression volley is imprecise.

failureEffect.narrative: The approach funnels you into the Brutes' shove range as designed. They reset before you reach the shelf.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot force the pace required to prevent the Brutes from setting their shove sequence.

stages.title: The Ley Line Altar
stages.description: The valley's ley line anchor is a ritual stone at the shelf's center, old enough to predate the Verdant Word by two centuries. Choir-Marshal Veth commands from it directly — not because she understands the binding ritual, but because standing on the stone gives her a live feed of the flood's advance across the whole valley. She has been reading that feed for five years. She knows exactly how much ground she controls. She intends to keep controlling it.

preCombatOpportunities.name: Place the Oath Stone at the Anchor

successEffect.narrative: The oath stone carries the original binding frequency for this valley's anchor. When you place it at the ritual stone, it creates interference in Veth's altar connection — not breaking it, but making the feed unreliable. She loses positional certainty across the valley for roughly ten seconds. The Tide Lashers, operating on her directed coordination, lose their instructions in that window and disengage.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no instrument resonant with the original binding frequency.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The altar does not respond to general approach. Malacor's connection to the Sanctum holds.

successEffect.narrative: Veth's opening is a sustained tidal_grasp setup — she needs two seconds on the altar stone to lock the flood's feed before committing to combat. A party that closes the distance before she completes the setup forces her off the stone. Veth's first action becomes a direct attack without the positional read. She spends her second action re-establishing the altar connection. You cost her the setup and the first turn.

failureEffect.narrative: Veth completes the altar connection before you can close. She enters the fight with full positional read on the valley.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Veth finishes the setup. The ley line feed is active when combat begins.

name: The Archive of Broken Seals
description: A sealed sub-basement beneath the Whispering Athenaeum where the High Council stored the original vote record of the Verdant Word's dissolution — not the official record, the real one. The official record says the Order dissolved peacefully. The real record is down here, behind fifty years of active suppression, maintained by the councillor who wrote the false version and has been tending the seals ever since.

lore.text: The dissolution vote was not unanimous. The official record shows consensus. The real record, sealed in this archive, shows three dissenting votes — councillors who refused to ratify the pact with the Architect and were killed for it before the vote could be annulled. Their names are on the sealed record. They are not on the monument in the Capital. The monument was commissioned by the same councillor who is currently maintaining the archive's seals.

The seals are not just hiding the vote. They are suppressing the residual magic of the three dissenting councillors — each dissent was a spoken refusal, and spoken refusals carry binding weight in Verdant Word law. Three active refusals, fifty years old and still running, are doing measurable anti-Fraying work. The High Redactor is not maintaining the seals to hide the truth. He is maintaining them because releasing the dissents would accelerate the Fraying's exposure — and he made a promise to the Architect that exposure would not happen.

The High Redactor's name does not appear in the official dissolution record because he wrote it. He removed himself from the historical record the same way he removed the dissenting votes — carefully, with attention to what would be checked against what. He has been here for fifty years maintaining a document that proves his own guilt, because destroying it would release the dissenting councillors' magic uncontrolled into the Fraying. He is trapped by his own thoroughness.

stages.title: The Outer Stacks
stages.description: The archive's entrance level is exactly what an archive looks like — rows of iron-bound ledgers, clerks' desks with quills still in the inkwells, filing systems organized by date and signatories. The Seal Wardens moving between the stacks are doing what they were assigned to do: ensuring nothing is removed without authorization. The fact that the authorization protocol ended fifty years ago has not registered as a reason to stop.

successEffect.narrative: The Athenaeum Scroll carries the reading authorization frequency the Seal Wardens were programmed to recognize. Two of them recategorize you as cleared personnel and resume their patrol routes. The Redactors are not governed by reading authorizations — they are governed by writing authorizations, which are different. They do not stand down.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no reading authorization. The Seal Wardens' access protocol begins.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The archive's outer stacks require reading clearance. You do not have it.

successEffect.narrative: The seals on the outer stacks' restricted shelves are still broadcasting the authorization signature of the councillor who applied them. An arcane-attuned party can read the signature directly and identify which Redactor is anchored to which seal — and which seal, if acknowledged as legitimate by a party that knows what it is, causes the anchored spirit to process the acknowledgement as a review request. One Redactor enters review protocol and exits the combat zone. He will be back. But not before you have cleared the outer stacks.

failureEffect.narrative: The seal signatures require arcane attunement to read. The Redactors' anchors hold.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot identify the seal signatures. The archive's suppression field holds full strength.

stages.title: The Restricted Vaults
stages.description: The second level is where the authorization tiers change. The open stacks gave way to iron-doored vaults, each one sealed with a different councillor's signature. Vault Guardians hold the corridor intersections — they are not archivists, they are locks. The Redactors here are the ones assigned to the dissolution records specifically, and their silence application is not a combat tactic. It is their job.

successEffect.narrative: The vault system was designed to admit Verdant Word personnel at the priesthood level. The seal carries that authorization. One Vault Guardian receives the credential and runs its access check — the check finds the seal valid, cross-references against the cleared personnel list, notes the personnel list has not been updated in fifty years, and defaults to granting access. It steps aside. The other Guardian has not received the credential directly and does not default.

failureEffect.narrative: The vault system requires Verdant Word authorization for the restricted tier.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You carry no credential that reaches the vault's authorization tier. The Guardians hold their positions.

successEffect.narrative: The Vault Guardians' combat protocol requires a targeting lock before they can apply stone_skin — they are designed as a deterrent first and a weapon second. A party that advances without giving them a targeting window denies the stone_skin application. You are inside their engagement distance before they have established a defensive stance. The first exchange is on your terms.

failureEffect.narrative: The Guardians establish targeting locks before you reach engagement distance. Stone_skin activates.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot force the approach at the pace required. The Guardians have armorValue 16 and a functioning defensive protocol.

stages.title: The Sealed Chamber
stages.description: The deepest vault holds the original vote record — seven folios, each signed and witnessed, with the three dissenting votes still active and audible as a faint harmonic in the room's air. The High Redactor stands at the central table with all seven folios in front of him, not reading them. He has them memorized. He is maintaining them — applying the suppression seals by rote, the way a person breathes. He has been doing this for fifty years. He will not stop because you have arrived.

successEffect.narrative: The Athenaeum Scroll contains the names the true record holds — names the official record does not. Speaking them in this room activates the dissenting votes' residual magic. The Vault Guardian, anchored to the suppression field, receives the activation signal as a conflict between its two directives — suppress the record, and defer to valid Verdant Word authority. The conflict stalls it. It does not attack. It waits for the High Redactor to resolve the conflict. The Redactor cannot resolve it without acknowledging the names. He does not acknowledge them.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no record that carries the dissenting councillors' names.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The High Redactor has maintained these seals for fifty years. He is prepared for uninformed intrusion.

successEffect.narrative: The dissenting votes are still running as a harmonic in the vault's air — faint, fifty years old, but active. A party with sufficient harmony can key into the frequency and use it as a partial resonance anchor. The High Redactor's soul_shatter opener reads the party's stat profile by disrupting their harmonic baseline. A party already keyed into the vault's dissent-frequency has a baseline the soul_shatter cannot cleanly disrupt. The opener fires at reduced effect. He recalibrates. That costs him his first turn.

failureEffect.narrative: The harmonic is too faint for your baseline. The soul_shatter reads your full profile.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The dissent-frequency is inaccessible without harmonic attunement. The High Redactor opens with a complete assessment.

name: The Echoing Caravan
description: The Halfling Songkeepers' caravan is moving north toward the Spire, carrying the complete True Name of Lyra in the High Cantor's memory and in the voices of every singer walking behind him. The Tidebound know what the True Name does to the water spirit's connection to the Architect. They have sent assassins. The caravan's outer wardens cannot tell the difference between a Tidebound agent and an armed stranger who arrived unannounced. Neither can the Initiates protecting the wagons.

lore.text: The True Name of Lyra is not a name. It is a forty-seven verse song in the halfling modal tradition, where the melody carries the binding weight and the words are mnemonic scaffolding. The High Cantor learned it from his predecessor, who learned it from hers, in an unbroken chain stretching back to the original binding ceremony. Every link in that chain died carrying the song. The High Cantor is sixty-three years old and has been walking toward the Spire for nine months.

The Tidebound silencers sent to intercept the caravan were not briefed on what the Song does specifically — they were told to stop the caravan and ensure the High Cantor did not reach the Spire. Two of the assassins, having spent three days in proximity to the caravan before their attack, were observed humming. They did not notice they were doing it. The Song is not passive. It reaches into people who hear it long enough and begins the binding work without being asked.

The Songbook exists because the High Cantor made a decision forty years ago that he has never told the caravan about. He does not trust that the chain will hold. He is sixty-three years old and has no successor who has fully memorized all forty-seven verses. He wrote the Songbook as insurance, knowing that a song committed to paper is a song that can be sung by someone who was not born into the tradition. He has been carrying it, sealed, at the bottom of his wagon for four decades. He intends to hand it to whoever proves they are going to the Spire to finish what the binding started.

The Harmony Convergence is what the Song sounds like when the party stops fighting the caravan and the caravan stops treating the party as a threat. It lasts approximately ninety seconds. In those ninety seconds, the ambient Fraying within a half-mile radius of the caravan recedes by a measurable distance. The High Cantor notices. He has been measuring Fraying proximity for nine months. He says nothing. He adjusts his pace. He is walking slightly faster.

stages.title: The Wagon Approach
stages.description: The caravan's outer ring is Chorus Initiates and Verse Wardens — the youngest and the most experienced, which is the halfling way of deploying defence. The Initiates are protecting something they have been told is the most important thing in the world. The Tidebound Silencers moving through the outer wagons are already here, already working. The caravan has not identified them yet.

successEffect.narrative: The Oath Stone from the Shrine of the First Oath carries the binding frequency the Song was built to reinforce — the halflings recognize it immediately, not as an object but as a resonance. Two Chorus Initiates step back, their threat posture collapsing into something closer to reverence. The Verse Wardens do not stand down — they are not governed by resonance alone — but the Initiates' response reads to the Tidebound Silencers as a breach in the outer line. The Silencers shift focus. The Wardens notice the shift.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing the caravan recognizes as a binding instrument.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Chorus Initiates have been told to treat unknown armed arrivals as Tidebound agents until proven otherwise. You have not proven otherwise.

successEffect.narrative: The Song running through the caravan is not background music — it is an active harmonic field that every person within range is either resonating with or fighting. A party with sufficient harmony resonates. The Verse Wardens feel the alignment and pause their threat assessment. The Tidebound Silencers, whose entire profession depends on not being affected by the Song, register the party's resonance as a complication. They delay their strike to reassess. That delay is the opening.

failureEffect.narrative: You cannot align with the caravan's harmonic. The outer defence treats you as an unknown threat.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The caravan's Song requires harmonic attunement to engage non-violently. You do not have it.

stages.title: The Lead Wagon
stages.description: The lead wagon is where the High Cantor walks, which means it is where the Tidebound Silencers are concentrating their effort. The Verse Wardens ahead of the wagon have identified at least one Silencer and are fighting on two fronts — the party approaching from outside and the assassins already inside the defence perimeter. The Song is louder here. It is also more fragile.

successEffect.narrative: The Tidebound Silencers use shadow_step to move through the caravan's cover without being tracked by the halflings, who are not trained to read shadow magic. A party fluent in shadow technique can follow the shadow-trail and expose one Silencer's position to the Verse Wardens before the Silencer can complete their approach. The Wardens redirect immediately — they have been looking for exactly this. The Silencer has no shadow cover left. They disengage.

failureEffect.narrative: You cannot read the Silencers' shadow trails. They maintain their concealment.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Silencers' shadow movement is invisible without shadow attunement. The Verse Wardens are fighting blind.

preCombatOpportunities.name: Present the Seed of the First Tree

successEffect.narrative: The Seed of the First Tree carries the elven binding frequency that the halfling Song was designed to harmonize with — the two traditions came from the same original ritual. The Verse Wardens recognize the Seed's resonance as proof of origin. They stand down and redirect their attention to the Silencers entirely. The caravan's inner defence is now aligned against the correct threat. The Wardens are better at fighting people who are trying to stop the Song than they were at fighting everyone.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no binding instrument the halfling tradition recognizes as proof of shared origin.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Verse Wardens cannot distinguish between threats. Everyone who is not singing is a threat.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The High Cantor has been watching from the lead wagon. He has seen the Oath Stone. He has seen how the caravan responded to it. He opens the wagon door and looks at the party for a long moment. Then he says: 'You know what this is for.' He steps back inside. The door stays open.

stages.title: The Singer's Podium
stages.description: The High Cantor has stopped the wagon. He is standing on the driver's board with his hands at his sides, not singing. When he stops singing, the caravan goes quiet in stages — Verse Wardens first, then Initiates, then the ambient harmonic that has been running since he started walking nine months ago. The silence is not peaceful. He is deciding whether you are going to the Spire to finish what the binding started, or whether you are another delay. He has had a lot of delays.

successEffect.narrative: The sigil fragment carries the Architect's mark — the same mark the Song was assembled to unmake. When the High Cantor sees it, he closes his eyes for three seconds. When he opens them, he tells the Verse Wardens to stand down. He does not explain why. The Wardens stand down. The High Cantor looks at you the way a craftsman looks at a tool that is exactly right for the job. He has not looked at anything like that in forty years.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no evidence of what you intend to do at the Spire.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The High Cantor has been walking for nine months. He does not have time for parties who cannot prove their purpose.

successEffect.narrative: The High Cantor's assessment is direct: he asks where you are going and what you intend to do there. A party with sufficient conviction answers without hedging. He listens. He does not ask a second question. He resumes singing. The caravan resumes moving. The fight begins not with the High Cantor's attack but with his opening buff — and the buff is applied to the party, not his defenders.

failureEffect.narrative: The High Cantor hears uncertainty. He has been walking nine months toward certainty. He does not have patience for the other thing.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot project the conviction the High Cantor requires. He resumes the fight from where he left off.

stages.title: The Harmony Convergence
stages.description: The High Cantor steps out of the wagon and begins the forty-seven verses from the beginning. The caravan stops. Every singer who can still stand joins in. The Tidebound Silencers still inside the perimeter have been humming for three days without realizing it — when the Song starts in full, two of them stop fighting. The third does not. The third is the one who has been here for six days and has not yet started humming. She knows what is happening to her colleagues. She will not allow it to happen to her.

name: The Pass of the Rejected
description: Stone Tooth Pass revisited at higher stakes — the Ironvein Loyalists and Goldcrown Usurpers who refused to accept the Third Heir's coronation have formed the Rejected Alliance and are holding the pass against the new king's supply lines. They are losing the succession war and they know it. The Vanguard Captain holding the pass is not fighting for victory. He is fighting because letting the pass fall without a fight is the last thing he is not willing to do.

lore.text: The Rejected Alliance formed the day after the Coronation of Stone. It was not organized — it was simultaneous, like a reflex. Ironvein and Goldcrown soldiers who had been trying to kill each other the week before found themselves standing at the same pass with the same problem: they had both lost, and the only person who had won was a Third Heir they had both spent five years pretending did not exist. The alliance is not ideological. It is the only thing they had left in common.

The Vanguard Captain served under three Holds commanders before the succession crisis and was considered the most capable officer in the border garrison. His recommendation, filed six years ago, was that the succession dispute be resolved by coronation rather than by attrition. The recommendation was ignored. He is now commanding the faction that ignored it, holding a pass he recommended not holding, against an outcome he recommended pursuing. His quarterly reports to the Alliance command have become increasingly brief.

The pass itself is not just geography. It was the original border agreement site — the location where the Dwarven Holds formally recognized the Silver Vale's territorial boundaries three centuries ago. That agreement was witnessed by the Verdant Word, sealed with oath-magic, and is still technically active. The Rejected Alliance is garrisoning the site of a binding oath. Every day they hold it in opposition to the Third Heir's legitimate succession, the Fraying in the pass intensifies. The Captain knows this. His most recent report to Alliance command is two sentences long.

stages.title: The Frozen Approach
stages.description: The pass's lower approaches are held by Iron Conscripts running the same patrol patterns the border garrison used for decades — the patterns that the Vanguard Captain wrote, which he now uses against the army he once served. Siege Casters have repurposed the pass's original ward-stones as offensive emplacements. The ward-stones were put here to protect the boundary agreement. They are now protecting the people trying to invalidate it.

successEffect.narrative: The Heir's Signet carries the ancestral recognition that the Rejected Alliance's entire cause is built around denying. Two Iron Conscripts see it and go still. They are not defecting — they are processing a legal fact that contradicts their current orders. The processing takes long enough that they are out of position when the fight begins. The Siege Casters do not have the same legal problem. They have already committed to a position.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no succession credential. The patrol treats you as a supply-line threat.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The lower approach is held by soldiers running a specific patrol doctrine. You are on the wrong side of it.

successEffect.narrative: The ward-stones are oath-magic constructs — the same substrate fire disrupts throughout the Vale. One Siege Caster's emplacement goes down before it can establish its electrified suppression field. The Caster himself survives but is fighting from an exposed position with his primary tool gone. He pulls back to the secondary emplacements. The Iron Conscripts hold their line without the cover.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no fire skill. The ward-stone emplacements hold their suppression fields.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The emplacements are reinforced oath-stone. They will not come down without fire.

stages.title: The Tower Interior
stages.description: The pass's central tower is where the two factions' command structures have merged into something that is functionally military but philosophically incoherent — Ironvein and Goldcrown officers sharing a command post, deferring to a Vanguard Captain who served neither of them, running a siege that neither faction's doctrine was designed for. The Siege Casters here are the senior engineers. The Iron Conscripts are the veterans who chose the pass over the surrender.

successEffect.narrative: The Loyalist Seal was issued by the Ironvein faction command before the coronation — it is technically still valid as an Ironvein authorization. Two Ironvein conscripts read it as a command from their original chain, which supersedes the Rejected Alliance's joint command structure. They stand down and wait for clarification from the Ironvein officers. The Goldcrown conscripts watch this with the particular expression of soldiers whose coalition partner has just complicated the chain of command.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no credential the joint command structure recognizes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The tower interior's command structure does not have an entry for unauthorized parties.

successEffect.narrative: The Rejected Alliance's joint command has a structural flaw: it requires Ironvein and Goldcrown officers to agree before issuing orders, which takes longer than either faction's independent command structure. A party that advances with visible command presence forces the joint command to issue an intercept order. The order takes four seconds to authorize. In those four seconds, the Siege Casters hold for direction that has not arrived yet. You advance through the authorization gap.

failureEffect.narrative: The joint command issues the intercept order faster than you expected. The Siege Casters do not wait.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot exploit the command gap without projecting sufficient authority. The tower's defenders move on reflex.

stages.title: The Observation Deck
stages.description: The Vanguard Captain has been on the observation deck since before dawn. He is looking at the pass he wrote the defence doctrine for, held by soldiers following orders he issued, against an outcome he recommended. His most recent report to Alliance command said: 'The pass will not hold. Recommend negotiated withdrawal.' Alliance command's response arrived this morning. He has not opened it. He already knows what it says.

successEffect.narrative: The Captain's recommendation — coronation over attrition, six years ago — is on record. The Heir's Signet proves the recommendation was correct. Two Iron Conscripts, overhearing the exchange, stand down. They have been holding this pass for months. They have known for weeks it would not hold. They were waiting for someone to arrive who knew the Captain was right. The Captain says nothing. The Conscripts stand down. The fight is now between him and the party.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no succession credential that makes the Captain's recommendation relevant.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Captain did not open the Alliance command's response because he knew what it said. He does not need your assessment of the situation.

successEffect.narrative: Thrain's complete report documents the Tidebound's coordination of the succession crisis as a deliberate distraction — the Ironvein-Goldcrown war was not coincidental with the flood's advance. The Captain reads the relevant section in thirty seconds. He sets the report down. His first attack is delayed while he recalculates what he is actually defending. That recalculation costs him his opener. He still fights. But the fight has a different quality to it — he is no longer certain of his ground.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no documentation that contextualizes the succession crisis within the larger threat.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Captain does not reconsider his position without evidence. He has been in this pass for months. He requires specifics.

name: The Wound in the World
description: A corrupted Verdant Word shrine near the Spire of Silent Conviction where three hundred years of broken oath-residue has concentrated into something that is no longer passive. The Field of Shattered Vows showed the Fraying spreading. This is where it is being directed from. The Architect is not present here. But it is paying attention through whatever is.

lore.text: The shrine predates the Verdant Word by four centuries. It was built at the intersection of three ley lines by a tradition that no longer exists, for a purpose that the Verdant Word's records describe only as 'containment.' What it was containing is not recorded. The Order built their outer wall around it and used it as a meditation ground for three hundred years without investigating further. The investigation would have been useful.

The Fraying Heralds are not corrupted priests in the way the Sigil-Bearer's Keep housed corrupted priests. They volunteered. The distinction matters — the Keep's inhabitants were turned, which means the corruption is incomplete. The Heralds chose, which means the corruption has full purchase. The difference in combat is that the Keep's priests could still be surprised. The Heralds cannot. They know exactly what they are.

The Wound is not the Architect. The Wound is what the Architect has been building toward the surface from the inside, using three hundred years of broken oath-residue as construction material. The Architect is still at the Spire's core. What the party fights here is an extension of its attention — a probe, designed to collect data on whoever is coming before they arrive at the Spire. The Wound loses. The Architect does not. Everything the party does here is recorded.

stages.title: The Blackened Approach
stages.description: The shrine's outer grounds are where the oath-residue has been accumulating longest — three centuries of broken promises pressed into the soil until the soil changed composition. Field Oath Breakers drift between the standing stones in the patterns of whatever promises they were made from. Field Shadow Weavers move through them, applying coordinated pressure with the specific efficiency of something that has been doing this for fifty years.

successEffect.narrative: The sigil fragment carries the Architect's own coordination frequency. When held forward, the Oath Breakers receive it as a recall signal — not a strong one, and not one the Architect issued, but the frequency is correct enough that two of them drift toward the shrine's center before the Shadow Weavers can redirect them. The Weavers notice. They will be more careful about coordination signals from outside sources going forward.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing that speaks the Architect's coordination frequency.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Oath Breakers do not respond to external signals. They respond to the Wound.

successEffect.narrative: Oath Breakers are compressed promise-residue — structurally identical to the substrate fire disrupts throughout the Vale, just at higher concentration. Two come apart under direct combustion. The Shadow Weavers pull back. They have been here long enough to recognize fire as the primary threat to the shrine's outer layer. Their formation tightens. The outer approach is thinner.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no fire skill. The oath-residue holds its form.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Oath Breakers' density is too high for non-fire disruption. They hold.

stages.title: The Shrine Interior
stages.description: The shrine's inner chamber is where the three ley lines intersect — which is to say, where the Wound is deepest. Fraying Wraiths move between the columns in tight patrol patterns that the Field Shadow Weavers are coordinating. The Wound is visible from here as a darkness in the air at the chamber's center that is not quite a shape but has stopped pretending not to be one.

successEffect.narrative: The Architect's seal, turned against its own network, broadcasts a recall frequency at the coordination channel the Wound is using to direct the Wraiths. One Wraith receives it as a genuine recall and phases toward the shrine's exit before the Wound can correct the signal. The correction takes three seconds. In those three seconds, the Fraying Herald — who coordinates the Wraiths manually — has to choose between correcting the signal and maintaining his own positioning. He corrects the signal. He loses his positioning.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing that speaks to the Wound's coordination network.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The shrine's coordination network is self-correcting against external interference.

preCombatOpportunities.name: Advance Before the Herald Reads You

successEffect.narrative: The Fraying Herald's opening is soul_shatter — a full profile read that calibrates the Wound's pressure against the party's specific weaknesses. A party that closes the distance before the read completes denies the Herald his baseline. You advance immediately. The soul_shatter lands on partial data. The Herald's follow-up is calibrated for a party that does not exist. He recalibrates. That costs him two turns.

failureEffect.narrative: The Herald completes his profile read before you can close. The Wound receives the data.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot force the approach before the soul_shatter completes. The Herald has everything he needs.

stages.title: The Core Chamber
stages.description: The Wound is at the chamber's center. It is not a creature. It is not a person. It is the shape that three hundred years of broken promises make when they are compressed far enough and given sufficient attention from something that knows how to direct them. It has lord_of_decay because the Architect gave it the capacity to demonstrate the endgame. It is demonstrating. The Architect is watching the demonstration from somewhere else. It is taking notes.

preCombatOpportunities.name: Place the Oath Stone at the Ley Line

successEffect.narrative: The Oath Stone carries the original binding frequency for one of the three ley lines the shrine sits on — the first oath spoken here, three hundred years ago, still resonant in the stone. When placed at the ley line's intersection point, it creates interference in the Wound's construction. Two Echo Shards, anchored to that ley line's residue, dissolve when the interference disrupts their substrate. The Wound registers the loss. Its first action is recalibration, not attack.

failureEffect.narrative: You have nothing resonant with the ley line's original binding frequency.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Wound has been building here for three centuries. It is not disrupted by general approach.

successEffect.narrative: The Wound's ambient decay_aura is continuous — it does not wait for the fight to begin, it begins the moment the party enters the chamber. A party with sufficient harmony can maintain their baseline against the aura's first three ticks without the harmony reduction stacking to the point where drain_reserves becomes immediately fatal. You enter with your harmonic intact. The Wound's first action is lord_of_decay rather than drain_reserves. The order matters.

failureEffect.narrative: The decay_aura stacks before you can establish your footing. Drain_reserves fires at full effect on the first eligible target.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot resist the ambient aura at the chamber's center. The Wound's opening sequence runs as intended.

name: The Gates of Atonement
description: Captain Hrolf is breaking the Capital's gates open from the inside. He has been preparing this for weeks — pulling the mechanisms apart, identifying the Fraying's lock points, positioning his remaining guard where they can hold longest. The Fraying knows what open gates mean for the Architect's timeline. It is sending everything it has to re-seal them before the operation completes.

lore.text: Hrolf filed his resignation from the Royal Guard the morning he began the gate operation. He backdated it to the day the quarantine order was issued — the illegal one, from the Guildhall. His reasoning, written in the margin: an officer following an illegal order is not an officer. He had been an officer for thirty-one years. He spent the last week of that career making sure the thing he had done wrong could be undone.

The gates were sealed with three mechanisms: a physical bolt, a Royal Guard authorization lock, and a Fraying anchor the Guildhall added without telling the Guard. Hrolf defeated the first two in a day. The Fraying anchor has been the job for three weeks. The Fraying anchor is not a passive seal — it actively repairs itself when damaged. Hrolf has been damaging it and waiting for it to repair, mapping the repair pattern, looking for the sequence that doesn't close back.

The Atonement Knights who stayed with Hrolf are not there because he ordered them to stay. He gave them a direct order to leave. They stayed because they read his resignation letter, which he left on the duty desk rather than sealing it, and they agreed with his reasoning. There are eleven of them. That is not enough to hold the gates. They know that. Hrolf knows that. The party arriving is not a surprise to him. He left the side entrance unlocked.

The Inner Sanctum records show that the Guildhall's Fraying anchor on the Capital gates was not installed to protect the Capital. It was installed to prevent the Allied Vanguard from ever being able to use the Capital as a staging ground — a contingency, in case the Architect's advance failed and the alliance formed anyway. The Guildhall was not just complicit in the Fraying's rise. It was insuring against the Fraying's failure. Guildmaster Vane signed the installation order himself.

stages.title: The Outer Courtyard
stages.description: The courtyard between the outer wall and the gate itself is where the Fraying's first response has arrived — Fraying Sealers moving through the mechanisms Hrolf's guard has been working on, actively undoing the progress. Atonement Knights are holding the courtyard's three entry points. The gate mechanism in the courtyard's center is partially open. If it closes again before the party reaches the inner section, the operation loses a day.

successEffect.narrative: The Royal Guard insignia carries the authorization frequency the gate mechanisms respond to — the same frequency the Fraying Sealers are trying to suppress. Two Sealers receive the broadcast and their suppression work stalls while they attempt to counter it. In the window before they re-establish their frequency, the Atonement Knights at the nearest mechanism complete a full repair cycle. Two Sealers have lost their work and have to start over.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no authorization frequency. The Sealers' suppression work continues uninterrupted.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The gate mechanisms do not respond to general approach. The Sealers hold their suppression fields.

successEffect.narrative: The Fraying anchors the Sealers are using as work-points are oath-residue constructs — the same substrate fire disrupts throughout the Vale. Two anchor points combust before the Sealers can shift to backup positions. Without their anchor, two Sealers lose their suppression coherence and dissipate. The remaining Sealers pull back to anchor points Hrolf hasn't reached yet. That buys the operation time it did not have.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no fire skill. The Fraying anchors hold.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The anchors are oath-residue constructs. They will not come down without fire.

stages.title: The Gatehouse Approach
stages.description: The Void Walkers arrived after the Sealers — they are the Fraying's heavier response, sent when the Sealers reported that the operation was further along than expected. They are not trying to re-seal the mechanisms. They are trying to kill the Atonement Knights maintaining them. A gate operation with no operators is a gate operation that stops.

successEffect.narrative: Hrolf's standing orders contain the gate operation's tactical plan — the specific sequence he is using to defeat the Fraying anchor's repair cycle. A party that reads the sequence can identify which position the Void Walkers are targeting next and redirect an Atonement Knight to intercept before they arrive. One Void Walker finds its approach vector blocked by an armored soldier who was not there thirty seconds ago. It disengages to find a new approach. The approach it finds takes longer. That matters.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no operational map of the gate sequence. The Void Walkers' approach vectors are invisible to you.

fallbackEffect.narrative: Without Hrolf's tactical plan, you cannot anticipate the Void Walkers' targeting priorities.

successEffect.narrative: The Void Walkers' combat doctrine targets the mechanism operators first — they have identified that the gate operation fails if the Atonement Knights are eliminated. A party that steps between the Void Walkers and the operators before they can establish targeting locks forces them to re-identify their priority targets. You are standing where the operators were. The Void Walkers adjust. The adjustment costs them their first action.

failureEffect.narrative: The Void Walkers establish targeting locks on the Atonement Knights before you can interpose.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot project the threat presence needed to redirect the Void Walkers' targeting priorities.

stageBranches.overrideDescription: The Guild Seal resonates against the Fraying anchor in the gatehouse wall — the same frequency as the anchor's installation signature. Hrolf notices. He pulls you aside before the final stage. 'That seal shouldn't open that lock,' he says. 'The Guild installed the anchor. I always thought it was opportunism.' He looks at the seal. 'It wasn't opportunism.'

stages.title: The Final Mechanism
stages.description: Hrolf is at the last anchor point — the Guildhall's addition, the one that has been repairing itself for three weeks. He has found the sequence. He needs four minutes to run it. The Fraying has sent its full remaining response. Hrolf does not stop working to fight. He keeps working. The party holds the line.

preCombatOpportunities.name: Use the Guild Seal Against the Anchor

successEffect.narrative: The Guild Seal carries the installation frequency of the Fraying anchor — the authorization the Guildhall used to add it to the gate system without Royal Guard knowledge. Presented at the anchor point, it registers as an administrative removal order from the installing authority. Two Fraying Sealers, anchored to the Guild's installation, receive the removal signal and dissolve. The anchor's self-repair cycle stalls for the duration of the signal. Hrolf finishes the sequence in the window.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no credential that reaches the Guildhall's anchor installation.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Fraying anchor's self-repair cycle is keyed to the Guildhall's authorization frequency. You have nothing that speaks it.

successEffect.narrative: Hrolf does not acknowledge that the party is holding the line. He is running the sequence. The Void Walkers and Sealers know what four minutes of uninterrupted work means — they shift from tactical to desperate, abandoning formation to rush the anchor point. A party with sufficient conviction meets the rush without breaking. You do not hold a line. You become the line. Hrolf finishes the sequence.

failureEffect.narrative: The Fraying's response breaks through your position before the sequence completes.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot hold a static position against a coordinated rush without sufficient conviction. The anchor's sequence is interrupted.

stages.title: The Inner Sanctum
stages.description: Hrolf leads you through a side passage into the Gatehouse's administrative level — the room where the Guildhall's installation was authorized and documented. The records are still here. They are intact. The Fraying has not touched this room because the Guildhall's Fraying anchor was keyed to protect the evidence of its own installation. Hrolf reads the installation order. He reads it twice. Then he hands it to you and says: 'Take this to wherever you're going. Make sure it gets there.' The room has one guardian left — a Void Walker anchored to the records themselves, put here to ensure the documentation never left this room.

name: The Nexus of Broken Vows
description: A floating rift near the Marsh of Broken Oaths where three hundred years of oath-residue from across the Vale has been converging. Unlike the Field of Shattered Vows, which spread the Fraying outward, or the Wound in the World, which directed it, the Nexus simply concentrates it — a point where the accumulated weight of every broken promise has exceeded the landscape's capacity to contain it and has begun to physically warp the space. The Architect did not create the Nexus. It created itself.

lore.text: The Marsh of Broken Oaths produced the Nexus the same way a wound produces scar tissue — not deliberately, not by design, but because sufficient damage in a concentrated area generates its own response. The Verdant Word identified the Nexus formation forty years ago, classified the report, and assigned three senior priests to monitor it. All three reports from those priests are in the Archive of Broken Seals, sealed under the same authority as the dissolution vote. This was not a coincidence.

The Entropy Husks were Verdant Word priests who volunteered to enter the Nexus forty years ago when the monitoring reports went dark. They were sent to retrieve the three priests who had been assigned to watch it. They found them. The retrieval was not successful. The priests who went in to retrieve the monitors became part of the Nexus in a different way than the monitors did — the monitors were absorbed passively, over years. The retrieval team was absorbed in a day, because they had made an oath to bring the monitors back.

The Nexus Keeper is not a consciousness. It is a weight. The three hundred years of broken promises have reached a mass at this point that produces behavior that resembles intelligence — it prioritizes high-harmony targets because harmony is what binding magic runs on, and it has learned that destroying harmony reserves delays the rate at which new oaths can be made. It is not strategic. It is gravitational. Everything that enters the Nexus's range eventually falls toward it.

stages.title: The Rift Edge
stages.description: The Nexus's outer boundary is where the spatial warping becomes visible — the ground at the rift's edge is displaced by approximately three inches from where it appears to be, and the shadows run at different angles than the light sources justify. Void Remnants drift along the edge, too diffuse to be identifiable as specific broken promises but too coherent to be mere ambient Fraying. They have been here long enough to develop patrol patterns.

successEffect.narrative: The Oath Stone carries a binding frequency that is still coherent — a completed promise, resonant against the Nexus's concentration of broken ones. At the rift's edge, where the displacement is thinnest, the Stone's frequency creates a localized anchor point. Two Void Remnants, drifting through the displacement field, contact the anchor and encounter a promise that was kept. The interaction dissolves them. A kept oath is the one thing the Nexus cannot absorb.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing that resonates as a completed binding. The Nexus does not distinguish between you and the oath-residue.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The rift's edge displacement field treats everything as potential absorption material.

successEffect.narrative: Void Remnants are oath-residue at its most diffuse — dispersed enough that the concentration fire requires to disrupt denser constructs is sufficient to evaporate them entirely. Two come apart before they can coalesce into attack posture. The Shadow Weavers at the edge pull back. They are not afraid of fire. They are repositioning to where fire will be less effective. They have learned from every party that has reached this far.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no fire skill. The Remnants maintain cohesion.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Void Remnants' diffuse state makes them resistant to non-fire disruption.

stages.title: The Nexus Interior
stages.description: Inside the rift boundary, the spatial displacement is severe enough that the party's sense of distance becomes unreliable. The Entropy Husks work the interior in the patterns they learned as retrieval priests forty years ago — methodical, grid-based, the professional habits of people doing a job that destroyed them. Reality_fracture is not a tactic here. It is a symptom. The Nexus is doing it to the space; the Husks are channeling it.

successEffect.narrative: The Architect's seal carries a coordination frequency the Nexus recognizes as an authority signal — not because the Architect controls the Nexus, but because the Architect has been using the Nexus as a resource and has left its operational signature in the residue. One Entropy Husk, receiving the authority signal, interprets it as a recall and moves toward the Nexus's center rather than the party. The Husk is doing exactly what a well-trained retrieval priest would do when receiving a superior's signal. Forty years of absorbed professional habit.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing that speaks the Nexus's operational language.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Entropy Husks do not respond to external signals. They respond to the Nexus's gravity.

successEffect.narrative: The Nexus's reality_fracture effect works by disrupting the party's spatial coherence — it targets the harmonic baseline the party uses to maintain formation and position. A party with sufficient harmony can establish an anchor point that resists the fracture effect long enough to maintain formation through the initial assault. The Entropy Husks' opening sequence assumes a disrupted party. They have never encountered a party that held formation inside the Nexus. They do not have a contingency.

failureEffect.narrative: The reality_fracture effect disrupts your formation before you can establish an anchor. The Husks' opening sequence finds you out of position.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You cannot anchor against the Nexus's spatial displacement. The fracture effect proceeds as designed.

stages.title: The Core Weight
stages.description: The Nexus Keeper is at the rift's center — not elevated, not enthroned, simply present at the point of maximum concentration. It is the heaviest thing in the Vale that does not have a body. The spatial displacement around it is severe enough that distance becomes genuinely unclear. The party knows they are close when the Keeper's soul_shatter cycle begins. It always starts with a profile read. It has been doing this long enough that the profile is very accurate.

successEffect.narrative: The resonance node fragment carries the Architect's anchor frequency from the First Hand encounter — a frequency the Nexus recognizes as originating from outside its own structure. When presented at the core, it creates a directional signal the Void Remnants interpret as a boundary. They drift toward the signal rather than the party. The Keeper runs its profile read without their contribution. The read is slightly less complete. In a fight governed by data quality, that matters.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing that creates a directional signal the Remnants will follow.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Void Remnants respond to the Keeper's gravity. Nothing external competes with it at this range.

successEffect.narrative: The First Hand Shard carries the Architect's calibration data from its encounter with the party — the profile the First Hand compiled before dissolution. When presented at the Nexus core, the Keeper receives the existing profile and does not need to run its own soul_shatter cycle. It already has the data. This means the Keeper's first action is lord_of_decay rather than the profile read. Lord of decay is more dangerous than soul_shatter in isolation. But the Keeper has skipped its setup turn, and the party has not.

failureEffect.narrative: You have no prior Architect profile data. The Keeper runs its own soul_shatter cycle.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Keeper profiles every incoming party from scratch. You are not an exception.

name: The Vanguard's Last Camp
description: The Allied Vanguard's encampment at the Spire's foothills — Krog's Stone-Tusk clan, the Third Heir's dwarven vanguard, the High Cantor's halfling singers, all in one place for the first time, the night before the march. The Architect has been watching the alliance form for months. It has assembled a targeted strike designed not to destroy the army but to sever its command — kill the three faction leaders, collapse the coordination, and leave an army that will not march without someone to march behind.

lore.text: The Architect's strike plan was precise: three simultaneous assassination attempts, timed to arrive in the camp's quietest hour, each one designed around the specific defensive patterns of its target. Krog's tent was approached from downwind, because the Architect had been monitoring the camp long enough to know the Orcs rotate their scouts based on wind direction. The Third Heir's tent was approached from the dwarven command side, because the Heir's guard was trained to watch for Ironvein and Goldcrown agents. The High Cantor's wagon was approached with silence, because the halflings' primary early warning is sound. The Architect had studied all three.

The Fraying Assassins the Architect sent were not random constructs. They were assembled from the camp's own oath-residue — the accumulated weight of every promise made inside the Vanguard's alliance, every contract signed, every commitment offered. The Architect used the alliance's own binding magic as construction material for the things trying to unmake it. The party that fights them is, in a specific sense, fighting the alliance's own promises turned hostile.

Krog survived the assassination attempt because he was not in his tent. He had been awake since midnight walking the camp perimeter — not on duty, not by order. He said afterward that he could not sleep because the contract was almost finished, and a contract almost finished is the most dangerous kind. The Third Heir survived because her dwarven guard dog, a veteran of the Coronation of Stone siege, bit the first Fraying Assassin that entered the tent before it could reach her. The High Cantor survived because he was singing. He is always singing.

stages.title: The Outer Perimeter
stages.description: The first Fraying Assassins have already breached the outer perimeter when the alarm goes up — they moved through the camp's patrol gaps with the precision of something that has been watching those gaps for weeks. Stonetusk Plain-Runners are running intercept. The Null Herald coordinating the strike is at the camp's edge, maintaining the Assassins' approach vectors.

successEffect.narrative: The Blood-Oath Token carries the Stone-Tusk's binding frequency — the same magic the Fraying Assassins were assembled from. The Assassins targeting the Orcish side of the camp receive the token's frequency as a contract-completion signal: the promise that made them has been honored, which means their construction substrate is being claimed by the completed oath rather than the broken one. Two dissolve. Krog, watching from the perimeter, sees this happen. He says nothing. He is watching with the expression of someone who has just understood something important.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry no binding frequency that reaches the Assassins' construction substrate.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Fraying Assassins are not responsive to external signals at approach distance.

successEffect.narrative: Thrain's complete report documented the Tidebound's intelligence-gathering methods, which the Architect has been using since the Tidebound's advance began. A party that has read the report recognizes the camp approach pattern immediately — the same gap exploitation, the same timing, the same wind-direction awareness. You know where the next approach vector is before the Assassin using it does. The camp's Plain-Runners are redirected to the correct position. The Null Herald's coordination signal hits a prepared defense.

failureEffect.narrative: You cannot anticipate the Assassins' approach vectors without the intelligence data.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The camp's perimeter defense is responding to contact rather than anticipating it. The Null Herald's coordination advantage holds.

stages.title: The Command Tents
stages.description: The command tents are where the three faction leaders are — Krog at the Orcish tent, the Third Heir at the dwarven command post, the High Cantor at the lead halfling wagon. Fraying Assassins are inside the perimeter, moving toward all three simultaneously. Stonetusk Bone-Guards are holding the outer tent lines. The Null Herald's second wave is already inside.

preCombatOpportunities.name: Use the Songbook Against the Assassins

successEffect.narrative: The Songbook of Lyra carries the True Name's binding frequency — the forty-seven verse song that reaches into people who hear it long enough and begins the binding work without being asked. The Fraying Assassins were assembled from the camp's oath-residue, which includes the halfling singers' binding work. The True Name resonates against its own construction material. Two Assassins targeting the halfling wagon stop, process the frequency, and dissolve — not from destruction, but from completion. The promise they were made from has been answered.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing that resonates with the Assassins' halfling-origin construction substrate.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Fraying Assassins' construction material does not respond to general harmonic approach.

successEffect.narrative: The Heir's Signet carries the ancestral recognition frequency that the dwarven binding oaths run on — the same substrate the Fraying Assassins targeting the dwarven command were assembled from. Presenting it at the command post activates the dwarven ancestral ward protocols, which the Architect did not know existed because they were sealed after the succession crisis. The Null Herald coordinating the dwarven-side assault receives an unexpected ward signal and breaks contact to reassess. The reassessment takes longer than the strike window allows.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing that activates the dwarven ancestral protocols.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Null Herald's coordination advantage holds without an active ward signal to counter it.

stages.title: The Severance
stages.description: The Severance arrived while the party was clearing the perimeter. It came for Krog specifically — the Architect calculated that the Stone-Tusk contract is the alliance's load-bearing promise, because it is the only one with three hundred years of binding precedent behind it. Krog is standing in the open at the camp's center, axe out, watching it approach. He has been waiting for it since midnight. He is sixty-one years old and he has fought in twelve contracts. He says: 'I have been waiting for you. You are between me and the morning.' He steps aside for the party. 'You first.'

successEffect.narrative: The Capital Key carries the authorization frequency of every Royal Guard officer who held command of the Capital's defenses — including the ones who gave the wrong orders. The Fraying Assassins assembled from the Capital's gate district oath-residue receive the Key's frequency as a command authority signal. They are not governed by it the way a living soldier is, but the frequency disrupts their coordination with the Severance long enough that two lose their approach vectors and dissipate. The Severance continues without them. It does not need them.

failureEffect.narrative: You carry nothing that disrupts the Severance's coordination with the remaining Assassins.

fallbackEffect.narrative: The Severance does not require the Assassins to function. They are a convenience, not a dependency.

successEffect.narrative: Krog watches the party step forward. He gives a single nod — the specific nod of a person who has just upgraded their assessment of someone from 'adequate' to 'sufficient.' He does not join the fight. He stands at the party's left shoulder, not attacking, not retreating — present. The Severance, which was calibrated for a party alone against it, runs its profile read and finds two conviction signatures instead of one. It recalibrates. The recalibration costs it its opening soul_shatter. Krog says: 'Contract almost finished.'

failureEffect.narrative: Krog watches. He does not step forward. The Severance profiles a party alone.

fallbackEffect.narrative: You do not project sufficient conviction for Krog to commit his presence. He holds his position.
